title: Cardiac 3D Mechanical and Electrical Signal Reconstruction via Defocused Speckle Imaging
authors: Ming Xia, Lin Liu, Ningbo Zhao, Zi Luo, Caifeng Shan, Wenjin Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Defocused speckle imaging (DSI) enables non-contact measurement of mechanocardiography (MCG) signals, such as seismocardiography (SCG) and gyrocardiography (GCG). As cardiac function is inherently governed by the coupling between mechanical motion and electrical excitation, this intrinsic relationship suggests that mechanical signals captured by DSI may further support the inference of electrical activities, i.e., the electrocardiogram (ECG). Therefore, we propose CardioNet, a physics-driven framework that leverages speckle motion signals together with physical model-based priors to reconstruct cardiac mechanical and electrical activities simultaneously. By coupling the MCG representations with the synchronized ECG, CardioNet captures the intrinsic electromechanical relationship of the heart, enabling non-contact and comprehensive cardiac monitoring. CardioNet was evaluated in a cross-subject study involving 20 laboratory subjects and 30 clinical patients, yielding Pearson correlation coefficients of 0.718/0.911 (SCG/ECG) in laboratory settings and 0.725/0.850 in clinical settings. Furthermore, it accurately captures fine-grained biomarkers, achieving mean timing errors of only 1.36/1.39 ms (lab) and 2.20/2.27 ms (clinic) for detecting aortic valve opening events in MCG signals and R-peaks in ECG signals, with similarly low timing errors for other hemodynamics-related markers. While promising for rhythm analysis, capturing subtle morphologies and spatial dynamics remains challenging. Future work will target 12-lead spatial reconstruction and broader pathological cohorts. To facilitate reproducibility, our dataset has been released at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/contactless-healthcare/CardioNet</uri>

title: GVHMR: Gravity-View Coordinates for Global Human Motion Recovery From Monocular Videos_supp1-3699758.pdf
authors: Sida Peng
publish: N/A
abstract: We present a novel method for recovering world-grounded human motion from monocular video. The main challenge lies in the ambiguity of defining the world coordinate system, which varies between sequences. Previous approaches attempt to alleviate this issue by predicting relative motion in an auto-regressive manner, but are prone to accumulating errors. Instead, we propose estimating human poses in a novel Gravity-View (GV) coordinate system, which is defined by the world gravity and the camera view direction. The proposed GV system is naturally gravity-aligned and uniquely defined for each video frame, largely reducing the ambiguity of learning image-pose mapping. The estimated poses can be transformed back to the world coordinate system using camera rotations, forming a global motion sequence. Additionally, the per-frame estimation avoids error accumulation in the auto-regressive methods. Experiments on in-the-wild benchmarks demonstrate that our method recovers more realistic motion in both the camera space and world-grounded settings, outperforming state-of-the-art methods in both accuracy and speed. We further introduce a Stationary Label Predictor (SLP) to estimate the motion stationary states of hands and feet. By producing sharper decision boundaries, the module mitigates unnatural artifacts such as foot-sliding and shows improved performance under fast-motion inputs. The code is available at <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://zju3dv.github.io/gvhmr</uri>.

title: Vector-Space Global Collocation Method for Modeling Rod-Driven Parallel Continuum Manipulators_supp2-3694012.mp4
authors: Rui Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Parallel continuum manipulators (PCMs) have attracted increasing attention owing to their flexibility, high precision, and strong load-bearing capacity, compared with their single-arm continuum counterparts. In this article, a kinetostatic modeling framework for PCMs is developed based on Cosserat rod theory, and a vector-space global collocation method (GCM) is proposed to solve the derived model. The GCM selects appropriate basis functions to approximate the unknown functions in vector space under different loading and actuation conditions. In particular, variables originally defined on the Lie group, such as rotation matrices, are reformulated entirely in the vector space while preserving orthogonality, thereby avoiding complex mathematical and numerical operations on the manifold. The proposed method is validated through FEM and experimental tests on a 3-rod PCM prototype. The maximum relative error in the rotation angle is 2.69%, and the absolute error in the end-effector height variation is 1.1 mm, corresponding to 0.55% of its initial height. Finally, a conceptual flexible vector thruster is demonstrated, within which a PCM-based thruster serves as a core component. The kinetostatic and compliance characteristics of this PCM-based thruster are analyzed using the proposed GCM-based semianalytical framework highlighting the simplicity, efficiency, and robustness of the developed framework.

title: Condition-Aware Progressive Knowledge Distillation: Lightweight and Accurate Prediction of Lithium-Ion Battery Remaining Useful Life
authors: Penghua Li, Ao Chen, Yunhong Che, Yangming Zhang, Jingjing Zhou, Xinmin Zhang, Guodong Wang, Xiaosong Hu, Schahram Dustdar
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: This study presents a condition-aware progressive knowledge distillation (KD) framework for developing lightweight models capable of accurately predicting the remaining useful life (RUL) of lithium-ion batteries. Within this framework, a teacher model is constructed by embedding a conditional variational autoencoder (CVAE) into a Mamba backbone. Through high-order compression and reconstruction of local–global battery features and the adoption of a dependency-aware loss function, the latent space is regularized to capture the underlying structure of degradation trajectories, enabling high-precision RUL prediction. Subsequently, a lightweight student predictor, realised as a CVAE enriched with operating-condition descriptors, is configured as the target model. Unsupervised clustering is employed in conjunction with a dual-path gating mechanism to activate an operating-condition monitor. While a multi-channel topology constructed from depthwise separable convolutions is triggered under the synergistic action of the gating weights to extract discriminative features across varying operating regimes and to deepen the representation of complex degradation dynamics. To transfer domain knowledge, a stepwise parameter-freezing KD strategy is devised whereby accuracy-controlled degradation knowledge is progressively distilled from the teacher, ensuring effective RUL prediction by the compact student. Validation indicates that the distilled lightweight model achieves average root-mean-square errors of 0.0163 on the MIT dataset and 0.0384 on a real-world EV dataset, while using only 0.167M parameters and requiring 13.88 ms per inference on embedded hardware. The code and models are available at https://github.com/Lipenghua-CQ/CPKD.

title: Fractional-Order Soft Actor Critic for Vision-Based UAV Navigation with Structured Experience Replay
authors: Tao Xu, Chaoyue Chen, Dong Liu
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Deep reinforcement learning holds significant potential for continuous control tasks, but its real-world application is often limited by algorithmic instability and poor sample efficiency. Although Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) is a strong and widely adopted baseline for continuous control, its standard temperature adaptation and uniform replay mechanism may limit training stability and data utilization in complex vision-based navigation tasks. To overcome these limitations, we introduce the Fractional-order Temperature Adaptive Soft Actor-Critic (FSAC) framework. FSAC integrates two core innovations: a fractional-order temperature controller that uses Grünwald-Letnikov derivatives to incorporate historical gradient information, thereby stabilizing entropy tuning and damping oscillatory dynamics; and a quadrant-based experience replay (QER) strategy that jointly rank-normalizes transition Q-values and temporal-difference errors to construct balanced mini-batches, improving sample informativeness and distributional diversity without relying on hyperparameter-sensitive prioritization. Experiments on OpenAI Gym benchmarks show that FSAC improves stability and performance in most settings. Furthermore, when deployed with a curriculum learning strategy in high-fidelity AirSim simulations for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) navigation across simple, dense, and dynamic obstacle environments, FSAC exhibits improved training stability and generalization performance, particularly in dynamic scenarios. These results indicate that FSAC provides a robust and efficient extension of SAC for complex, non-stationary control problems.

title: Fog-Edge Enabled Internet of Sound Framework for Integrated Consumer Healthcare Systems
authors: Syed Rizwan Hassan, KangYoon Lee
publish: IEEE Transactions on Consumer Electronics
abstract: The Internet of Sound (IoS) paradigm enables networking of sound devices for sensing, processing, and transmission of acoustic information. Integrating advanced acoustic sensor technologies and signal processing techniques can provide a suitable platform for implementing advanced applications. The phonocardiogram (PCG) has been extensively utilized in the diagnosis of coronary heart disease (CHD), one of the major causes of death. This research presents a novel Fog computing-enabled IoS-based (FIoS) framework that processes patients' heart sounds (HS) for real-time cardiac disease diagnosis. Furthermore, this research proposes a rate-aware node selection algorithm (RANSA) that accounts for edge nodes' sensing rates when connecting to fog devices. Finally, an adaptive resource-based clustering algorithm (ARBCA) is designed to provide efficient resource utilization in dynamic computing environments. Evaluations performed against the traditional FIoS baseline demonstrate that the proposed RANSA reduces latency by 7.22%, energy consumption by 0.82%, network consumption by 26.44%, and computational cost by 32.64%, while ARBCA reduces latency by 95.0%, energy consumption by 11.54%, network consumption by 78.69%, and computational cost by 77.4%, highlighting its optimum performance.

title: Explainable Multimodal Intelligence for Adaptive Identity Document Fraud Detection
authors: Sameer Sethi, Kinshuk Kumar, Parisha Khurana, Preeti Kaur
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: Identity document verification systems are increasingly susceptible to complicated presentation attacks (PAs) such as copy-move fraud and facial morphing. The current visual-based deep learning methods are highly unreliable and difficult for explanation. In this paper we present a transparent multistage framework to identify identity document fraud from structured metadata and document images side-by-side. The architecture combines an image encoder based on EfficientNet with a metadata encoder using BiLSTM, which is connected by deep feature fusion. It is fine-tuned via a 2 -step finetuning scheme. Experimental results on the synthesized dataset demonstrate that our approach achieves better performance in all types of attacks with standard PAD metrics. Grad-CAMs also contribute to transparency and forensic interpretability for tooltips.

title: InfraShip-Net: An Efficient Deep Learning–Based Algorithm for Maritime Infrared Ship Detection
authors: Jiawei Liu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Accurate detection of maritime infrared ship targets is critical for maritime safety monitoring and autonomous maritime systems. However, ship targets in infrared maritime images often exhibit large scale variations, small object sizes, low contrast, and are easily affected by sea-surface reflections and environmental noise, making reliable detection challenging. To address these issues, this paper proposes InfraShip-Net, an improved YOLOv12-based detection framework. The model integrates a Multi-scale Semantic Marine Fusion Module (MSMF) for enhanced multi-level feature fusion, a Sea-Background Guided Attention mechanism (SBGA) to suppress sea clutter and highlight ship regions, and a Remote Weak-signal Enhancement Unit (RWEU) to improve detection of distant small ships. Experimental results on a public maritime infrared dataset demonstrate that InfraShip-Net achieves an mAP of 96.7% while maintaining real-time performance, significantly outperforming the baseline YOLOv12 model.

title: A CNN-BiLSTM Model with Attention and Gated Fusion for Uyghur Text Classification
authors: Jiahui Wang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Uyghur, as a typical low-resource agglutinative language, faces significant challenges in text classification, including limited annotated corpora, rich morphological variations, and difficulties in semantic modeling. To address these issues, we construct a 19,648-sample Uyghur news corpus with preprocessing steps including stemming and stop-word filtering, and learn word embeddings via Word2Vec. We then propose a CNN-BiLSTM model with attention mechanisms and gated fusion, where CNN extracts local features, BiLSTM captures global contextual representations, and a gated fusion module dynamically balances the two. Experimental results show that our model achieves 91.84% accuracy and 89.97% F1-score, outperforming competitive baselines including TextCNN, BiLSTM, RCNN, and CNN-BiLSTM. Ablation studies further confirm the contribution of each component. This work provides a reference for text classification in Uyghur and other low-resource languages.

title: Predictive Analytics of Adolescent Behavioral Patterns for Mental Health Forecasting
authors: Lingya Huang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Low-Altitude Airspace and Artificial Intelligence (LAAI)
abstract: Mental health issues have become a pressing global concern, affecting a growing number of adolescents. Many individuals experience psychological distress without timely recognition, as traditional assessment methods relying heavily on subjective self-reports that often suffer from bias, delayed detection, and underreporting. This gap leads to exacerbated mental health deterioration, impacting quality of life, academic performance, and social functioning, underscoring the urgent need for objective, real-time prediction tools. The study addresses this need by developing a multimodal machine learning framework for early mental state forecasting, with significant implications for enabling proactive interventions and alleviating healthcare burdens. The study utilizes a dataset that integrate diverse mental health factors including demographics, stress triggers, performance metrics (CGPA), satisfaction scores, and lifestyle data. For methodology, the study first preprocesses data through normalization, missing value imputation, and feature extraction to identify behavioral patterns. Then, the study compares four types of machine learning models: logistic regression, support vector machine (SVM), random forest, and XGBoost. Evaluates the model performances based on accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. Preliminary results show the proposed framework outperforms baseline models, with XGBoost achieving the highest AUC-ROC of 0.9610, demonstrating strong capability in distinguishing mental health states. These findings validate the potential of multimodal data and optimized machine learning algorithms in mental health prediction. Looking forward, the study aims to expand datasets with more diverse populations, refine feature engineering, and enhance real-world applicability, striving to develop a scalable system that improves adolescent mental health monitoring and intervention efficacy.

title: Infrared-Based Human Action Recognition Under Low-Light and Occlusion Conditions
authors: Aditya Balodi, Neeraj Kumar Pandey, Manoj Diwakar, Sanjay Roka, Aditya Joshi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The multimodal human action recognition framework being proposed in this study uses Infrared (IR) and skeletal motion clues along with a complementary fusion pipeline. The proposed system uses the ActiVit Transformer to get the high-level spatiotemporal features from the raw video sequences, taking advantage of its multi-head attention and hierarchical temporal aggregation to represent the long-range visual dependencies with reduced redundancy. At the same time, the skeleton sequences are being handled by the HPI-GCN-RP, a very accurate graph convolutional network that is augmented with relation-preserving topology modeling, and this makes it possible to capture joint interactions and structural motion patterns very well. The different representations created by the two modalities are mapped into a common latent space and then combined via a learnable feature integration module that has been specifically constructed to keep the cross-modal correspondence. The integrated representation is then used for the final action classification, which allows the system to take advantage of both appearance-based dynamics and pose-driven motion signatures. The results of the experiments confirm that this fused architecture is capable of stronger generalization and better discriminability when compared to single-modality baselines, especially in situations where complex motion, occlusion, or viewpoint variation is present.

title: Microstructure Reconstruction of Porous Materials via a Conditional Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Model with Soft and Hard Data
authors: Hai Mei
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Reconstruction of porous material microstructures is essential in fields like geology and materials science. Although deep learning has advanced this area, achieving high-quality and highly controllable reconstructions remains challenging. We propose a conditional soft- and hard-data denoising diffusion probabilistic model (CSHDDPM) that integrates soft and hard condition data into the multi-scale networks of diffusion models. This enables comprehensive feature understanding and generates diverse, high-quality results that satisfy condition constraints. Experiments on Berea and Beadpack datasets demonstrate that CSHDDPM outperforms other deep learning models in controllability and reconstruction quality.

title: P3LiteFormer: An Edge-Optimized YOLO-Transformer Hybrid for Small Object Detection in UAV Traffic Monitoring
authors: Amanda Gozho, Lin Bai
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Small object detection in UAV traffic imagery remains challenging because of limited resolution, dense scenes, and onboard computational constraints. To address this issue, this paper proposes P3LiteFormer, a lightweight YOLO-Transformer hybrid that introduces a Transformer encoder only at the high- resolution P3 feature layer of YOLOv8s. This scale-specific design enhances global contextual modeling for small objects while adding only 2.7% parameters and 3.1% more FLOPs. On the VisDrone2019 dataset, the proposed method achieves an mAP@0.5 of 0.404, improving the baseline YOLOv8s by 21.7%, with 19.1% gains in both precision and recall. These results show that P3LiteFormer provides an effective and efficient solution for real-time UAV edge deployment.

title: Study on the Influence of Different Liner Structures on Jetting Projectile Charge Forming and Penetration Performance
authors: Wenfeng Guo, Yuting Liu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Perception and Autonomous Control (IPAC)
abstract: In order to optimize the design of shaped warhead and improve the effectiveness of anti-armor weapons, this paper explores the influence of three kinds of liner structures (with wall thickness as the core variable) of spherical shape, conical shape and cylinder-cone combined shape on the forming and penetration performance of Jetting Projectile Charge (JPC) through theoretical analysis and finite element numerical simulation. The results show that The spherical shaped liner with a radius of curvature of 50 mm only forms a complete JPC when the wall thickness is 1.6mm and 2.4mm. The conical shaped charge liner can form JPC in the range of wall thickness of 2.4 mm, 3.2 mm, 4.0 mm and 4.8 mm. Due to structural defects, only high-speed fracture jets are generated under all wall thicknesses, and JPC cannot be formed. In terms of penetration performance, two kinds of effective JPC have good penetration effect on concrete target, but there are differences in the penetration of steel target and aluminum alloy target. The penetration depth of JPC formed by spherical liner into steel, concrete and aluminum alloy targets is 90 mm, 218 mm and 141 mm, respectively. Compared with the steel target, the penetration effect of the concrete concrete target is increased by 140%, and the penetration effect of the aluminum alloy target is increased by 57%. The penetration depths of JPC formed by conical liners into steel, concrete and aluminum alloy targets are 57 mm, 184 mm and 126 mm, respectively. Compared with the steel target, the penetration effect of the concrete target is increased by 223%, and the penetration effect of the aluminum alloy target is increased by 132%. This study has certain reference significance for the study of the forming mechanism and armor breaking function of the shaped rod penetrator.

title: DEEP: Differentiable Explicit Projection for Deep Networks with Unitary Constraints
authors: Tianxiong Chen, Xinyuan He, Pengyu Wang, Kanghua Xie, Wei He, Yingguan Wang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Maintaining stability in deep and recurrent neural networks is essential, yet gradient vanishing and explosion remain major challenges. In this paper, we introduce DEEP (Differentiable Explicit Projection), a framework that enforces unitary constraints on network weights through a fully differentiable explicit projection mechanism. DEEP leverages the Björck iteration to efficiently project unconstrained weights onto the unitary manifold, preserving norms and inner products while remaining fully compatible with backpropagation. Unlike previous implicit or computationally expensive methods, DEEP achieves strict orthogonality with minimal computational overhead. We evaluate DEEP across multiple benchmarks, including random unitary matrix learning, long-sequence Copying Tasks, pixel-by-pixel MNIST, and convolutional networks on CIFAR-10, demonstrating that a small number of Björck iterations is sufficient to maintain numerical stability, improve training convergence, and enhance overall performance. These results highlight DEEP as a practical and general approach for enforcing unitary constraints, offering an effective trade-off between stability, efficiency, and predictive accuracy in deep learning.

title: Research on Exercise Recommendation Based on Knowledge Graph and Dynamic Policy Learning
authors: Leqi Li, Xiaoxiao Wang, Wenfeng Chen
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Traditional knowledge graph-based exercise recommendation methods primarily rely on static semantic matching, often neglecting the evolution of students’ dynamic cognitive states during the learning process. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a Knowledge Graph-enhanced Adaptive Dueling Double Deep Q-Network (KGA-D3QN) recommendation model. The model formulates the exercise recommendation task as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), aiming to maximize long-term cumulative pedagogical returns. Experimental results on the Algebra 2005 dataset demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances students’ knowledge states while maintaining competitive accuracy and recommendation diversity.

title: An Intelligent Energy Management Framework for Cost-Efficient Renewable Power Utilization in Smart Homes
authors: Swapnesh Taterh, Ashish Kumar Rastogi, Arun Kumar Singh, Yamini Sharma, Mukesh Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: With conventional forms of energy, especially coal, slowly getting exhausted, the whole world seems to be taking a spurt toward finding alternative forms of energy from renewables. The energies derived from the sun and wind have enormous potential, which remains unexplored even now. If harnessed optimally, they have the potential to provide a large chunk of energy for the world's increasing demand for electricity. Recently, advances in artificial intelligence and neural networks have made great strides toward embedding renewable energy into intelligent energy management systems that optimize consumption to reduce total cost. This paper outlines an innovatively designed energy management system for domestic energy consumption with embedded solar PV, wireless power transmission, and AI consumption pattern learning methods. The framework, under design, allows proportional utilizations of the solar energy generated at home while functional loading patterns are ascertained in real time within the home. It is designed to be functional independent of net grid consumption and generally aims to affect energy efficiency, reduced reliance on conventional sources, and lower electricity cost.

title: Classification and Detection of Poultry Disease from Chicken Fecal Images Using Deep Learning Techniques
authors: Neduncheziyan, Marie Suja J, Amrita Vijay V, Esther Sheebaa S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: There are poultry diseases that present serious problems in terms of global food security and the poultry industry due to their impact on the economy and increased death rate. Detecting such conditions at an early stage and with high precision is very important for the prevention of further damage; however, traditional methods usually take too much time and lead to mistakes. This research suggests the use of deep learning to automatically diagnose various poultry diseases based on the image analysis of chicken feces. DenseNet-121 neural network model with SE blocks showed excellent results when applied to classification with 97.75% accuracy. Moreover, for disease localization, YOLOv8 model was used and provided high values of Mean Average Precision and Precision-recall. Both models have been implemented in Streamlit and Flask applications.

title: Research on Photovoltaic Energy Storage Cooperative Scheduling and Energy Management Strategies Based on Reinforcement Learning
authors: Peizhi Liu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: When a high proportion of photovoltaic (PV) power is connected to the grid, its output fluctuates significantly, placing high pressure on energy storage regulation. Furthermore, rule-based control lacks sufficient adaptability. To address this, this paper proposes a PV-storage collaborative scheduling method based on reinforcement learning. The method treats the grid-connected PV-storage system as the research object, integrating PV output, load demand, electricity price signals, State of Charge (SOC), and prediction bias to form a time-series state. It utilizes time-series feature encoding, secure projection, and Lagrange constraint updates to achieve improvements in economy, stability, and security. Under the same experimental protocol, rule-based control, heuristic methods, and traditional deep reinforcement learning models are compared. Ablation experiments and perturbation studies are used to verify its effectiveness and robustness. Results show that this method performs better in terms of total operating cost, curtailment rate, peak-to-valley difference, and SOC violation rate. It can enhance PV absorption, achieve orderly charging and discharging of energy storage, and improve the smooth control capability of grid-connected power. This demonstrates that reinforcement learning can provide an effective intelligent energy operation approach for PV-storage collaborative scheduling. Ablation analysis shows that temporal encoding mostly enhances cost-related coordination, whereas safety prediction mostly guarantees feasibility; nonetheless, existing evidence has been restricted to a station-level simulated platform.

title: A Deep Learning System Based on YOLO for Finding Insects in Precision Agriculture
authors: Anant Raj, Swati Vashisht, Sandeep, Bhavya Yadav, Saurav Kumar, Siddhant Singh Somvanshi
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: Managing pests on farms is an important part of Food security and modern ways to protect crops. This study introduces a YOLO-based deep learning framework for realtime agricultural pest detection utilizing the YOLOv8 architecture Roboflow exported an annotated pest dataset that had five pest categories: weevil, caterpillar, grasshopper, slug, and snail. The suggested system's mean Average Precision (mAP@0.5) for all pest classes is 0.952. The AP scores for each class range from 0.849 for caterpillars to 0.995 for snails and grasshoppers. To make sure that the framework works well in a variety of field situations, It uses data augmentation methods, decoupled anchor-free detection heads, and multi-scale feature pyramid networks. Tests on Google Colab's GPU infrastructure show that the system works much better than baseline models like YOLOv5 and Faster R-CNN, which can process more than 60 frames per second in real time with good accuracy and speed. Some uses are automated systems for monitoring crops, drones for field surveillance, and platforms for precision agriculture, and smart farms can get help and advice. Future research will focus on expanding the framework to include additional crop and pest species, along with more advanced YOLO variants and getting it to work on edge devices with limited resources.

title: Bimodal Retinal Image Fusion Using CMPE-Enhanced Swin Transformer
authors: Ojas Shrivastava, Neeraj Kumar Pandey, Manoj Diwakar, Sanjay Roka, Aditya Joshi, Prabhishek Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The automated classification of retinal diseases is becoming an increasingly popular area of research as the world wide incidence of sight-threatening eye disorders continues to grow while there is a shortage of trained ophthalmologists to diagnose and treat these conditions. Fundus photographs provide the most valuable vascular information about the surface of the retina, and Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT) provides a much more detailed cross-sectional view of each layer in the retina. While the majority of current deep learning methods are based only on one type of data (i.e., they do not take advantage of the complementary properties of both types of images), there are some that use both types of data but do not utilize the full capabilities of each. The authors of this work propose a new bimodal deep learning framework based on the Cross-Modality Swin Transformer design for the classification of retinal diseases. By employing the cross-modality embedding method, the authors were able to fuse the features of both types of images at the time of training using a modified Swin Transformer backbone. Furthermore, a new Cross-Modality Positional Encoding module has been added to the transformer architecture so as to maintain the modality-specific contextual information in the transformer latent space. To evaluate the performance of this proposed approach, the authors conducted experiments on the OLIVES retinal dataset with a stratified train-validation split. The results of these experiments demonstrate that the method generates superior classification performance results compared with unimodal and conventional CNNs, thus validating the use of multimodal fusion with transformer networks for retinal disease diagnosis.

title: An Interpretable Efficient Framework for Real-Time Pearl Millet Disease Detection
authors: Sanjeev Thakur, Seema Rao, Dibyajyoty Beura, Kanchan Galiyan, Omdev Dahiya, Tanima Thakur, Amin Renees M V
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The pearl millet crop is highly nutritious, resistant to drought and is under a great threat of fungal infections such as the blast and rust, which may cause up to forty percent of the crop to be lost. The conventional types of diagnosis are typically founded on the time-taking process and manual one, on the experience of the professionals, which makes it inapplicable to the large scale agriculture. The proposed solution is of the state of the art, as the current work proposes: automated smart framework, which would be inefficient to monitor the millet. The model uses the EfficientNetB0 architecture that is enhanced with an Enhanced Predictive Intelligence (EPI) attention mechanism. Both channel- and spatially-based attention have been incorporated into the EPI module to accurately identify disease patterns and their precise loci on the surfaces of leaves, producing unambiguous and understandable results. The training of a dataset containing 2,847 carefully hand-labeled images, both healthy and infected, gave impressive performance statistics, with 95.79 percent accuracy, 95.82 percent precision, and 95.80 percent F1-score. Grad-CAM visualization is also more effective in improving the interpretability of models by illustrating the main influential area. Having a small size of 18.5MB and a latency of inference of 75 ms, the model can run on Internet-of-Things devices with ease, which is why it can be deployed on the edge in sustainable farming settings. This kind of implementation will enable early and focused interventions, which may in turn decrease the use of chemical pesticides by a third to a half, and the yield losses of about forty percent. The presented study explains the capacity of interpretable, deployable deep-learning solutions on resource-constrained hardware, thus developing precision agriculture and, in line with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals, especially those related to Zero Hunger, Climate Action, and Responsible Production.

title: DepreXAI: An Explainable Transformer Ensemble Framework for ICD-10 Based Depression Severity Classification from Social Media Text
authors: Reshu Tyagi, Nishant Tomar, Ritu Ranjan Chaubey, Piyush Chauhan, Paras Garg, Mohit Bohra
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: Depression is one of the most critical public health challenges worldwide, affecting over 260 million people. Despite increased awareness, early detection remains difficult due to social stigma and limited access to mental health professionals. Social media platforms, where users openly express emotions, provide valuable signals for early depression identification.

title: A Novel Framework and Dataset for Subtle Hand and Finger Movement Recognition in Workflow Analysis
authors: Aiman Khan Nazir, Lanfang Dong, Bei Hua, Qasim Jan, Kang Dong, Xiahan Feng
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Subtle hand-centric activity recognition is important for workflow analysis across various domains, such as analyzing daily office behavior, monitoring the standardization of assembly line operations, and enabling automated grading of students’ laboratory experiments. Consequently, this paper presents a novel Subtle Hand-Centric Activity Recognition with Duration Estimation (SHRDE) framework, which facilitates workflow analysis and productivity assessments. The SHRDE utilizes a sliding-window mechanism on MediaPipe streams and a skeleton-based activity recognition model. SHRDE continuously recognizes activities, their transition, and measures duration, which helps to analyze the workflow and individuals’ performance. Moreover, we introduce a new multi-view office activity dataset (OA5V4) to support fine-grained recognition of hand and finger movements. It covers 5 activities, which are captured across 4 viewpoints. SHRDE is evaluated on unseen subjects (i.e., cross-subject validation), and an accuracy of 97.33% is achieved. Moreover, it is also validated across different postures, lighting conditions, occlusion, and multi-person settings, demonstrating its robustness in complex scenarios.<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</sup>

title: Generalizing Vision-Language Models to Novel Domains: A Comprehensive Survey
authors: Xinyao Li, Jingjing Li, Fengling Li, Lei Zhu, Yang Yang, Heng Tao Shen
publish: ACM Transactions on Multimedia Computing, Communications, and Applications
abstract: None

title: Multivariate Regression Calibration for Inter-Rater Consistency in Hypertonia Evaluation
authors: Mya Verrett, Jiaxi Liu, Alyssa Wieand, Andrew Skalsky, Tse Nga Ng
publish: IEEE Sensors Letters
abstract: Clinical evaluations of neuromuscular disorders, such as hypertonia, have been based on perception and can vary from physician to physician. In efforts to provide an objective assessment a multimodal sensing glove was developed, measuring the force and motion trajectories, recording the muscle's resistance to evaluate severity. However, due to grip and evaluation speed differences, variations in the output magnitude between raters require calibration for effective cross comparison. In this work, we demonstrate a multivariate regression model to address inter-rater variability across clinicians. The model uses both the acceleration and force recorded from the glove to calibrate each rater's assessment. Additionally, it effectively increased the kernel density overlapping area between clinicians after calibration, reducing variability and increasing force output consistency. Ultimately, this work will enhance hypertonia monitoring accessibility to support rehabilitation specialists.

title: TVFI-GST: From Regime-Level Importance to Fusion-Aware Traffic Forecasting_supp1-3699780.pdf
authors: Jianming Zhan
publish: N/A
abstract: Forecasting the future state of a single target sensor from multi-sensor observations is a critical task in intelligent transportation systems, supporting congestion warning, signal control, and fine-grained traffic management at key locations. Existing methods under this setting often face three limitations: they characterize feature contributions only at the point or window level, rely on reduction-oriented feature processing that may discard complementary information, and rarely transform extracted structural information into relation-aware cues for downstream prediction. To address these issues, this paper proposes TVFI-GST, a spatiotemporal forecasting framework driven by time-varying structural cues. TVFI-GST first constructs target-semantic phase partitions and models phase-level temporal feature importance. It then employs fuzzy information granules (FIG) as carriers of trend evidence and reliability information to enable reliability-aware granular soft fusion, suppressing redundancy while preserving and reconstructing task-relevant information. Finally, the learned fusion structure is converted into dynamic relational cues and lag-aware context for fusion-aware prediction, forming a closed loop between representation learning and downstream forecasting. In this way, FIGs support phase-level importance modeling, reliability characterization, and information-preserving fusion, elevating temporal feature importance from a local explanatory signal to a stable structural interface for subsequent modeling. Experiments on six real-world traffic datasets demonstrate that TVFI-GST achieves strong overall forecasting performance against competitive baselines, while also offering interpretable structural cues under complex traffic conditions.

title: An Embedded High-Temperature LTCC Rogowski-Coil Current Sensor for SiC Intelligent Power Modules
authors: Pengyu Lai, Sudharsan Chinnaiyan, Peng Sun, Yuxiang Chen, Zhuowen Feng, Yue Zhao, H. Alan Mantooth, Zhong Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: This paper proposes a Rogowski-coil current sensor suitable for high-temperature operation and direct integration into SiC intelligent power modules (IPMs). The proposed current sensor is designed and packaged using low-temperature co-fired ceramic (LTCC) technology. Key parameters, including parasitic resistance, series inductance, mutual inductance and isolation voltage, are evaluated from 25°C to 200°C. The current capability is also characterized at 25°C. A 900 V SiC half-bridge power module integrating the LTCC current sensor was developed and experimentally characterized through double-pulse tests (DPTs) from 25°C to 150°C. The results confirm that the embedded sensor introduces negligible parasitic effects and switching losses while maintaining a linear and temperature-stable transfer impedance of 0.02 V/A. The demonstrated LTCC-based Rogowski-coil current sensor offers a compact, isolated and thermally robust solution for current measurement in SiC IPMs.

title: Pay-As-You-Go via Spectrum Bux: A New Paradigm for Dynamic Spectrum Access
authors: Naru Jai, Yi Shi, Huacheng Zeng, Yang Xiao, Wenjing Lou, Y. Thomas Hou
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: Traditional spectrum management has largely relied on exclusive spectrum assignments. With the escalating demand driven by emerging wireless technologies, spectrum has rapidly become a scarce resource, fundamentally constraining the deployment of new applications. To address this, advanced approaches such as auction-based licenses and shared spectrum access have been introduced to better accommodate growing demand. However, these methods are typically designed and tailored for specific spectrum bands, making them difficult to generalize across other bands. This limitation underscores the need for a new, unified spectrum management paradigm that can efficiently allocate spectrum across diverse frequency bands and wireless applications, while maintaining the flexibility to adapt to future demands. This article introduces ``Pay As You Go'' (PAYG) dynamic spectrum access and its enabling mechanism, Spectrum Bux (SB) - an innovative currency system, both originally envisioned by the U.S. National Science Foundation. We show how SB establishes both an economic and technical framework to enable short-term spectrum leasing and cooperative sharing, thereby enhancing the efficiency and flexibility of spectrum utilization. We demonstrate how SB addresses limitations of existing spectrum management approaches while fostering innovation and safeguarding critical wireless communication needs. Furthermore, we identify key open challenges in applying SB to dynamic spectrum allocation in PAYG and highlight promising directions for future research.

title: Time Series Forecasting of Customer Spend using Inception-Residual Convolutional Neural Networks
authors: Vanja Mileski, Jernej Vičič, Branko Kavšek
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: This paper addresses next-month customer spend forecasting in retail using a large-scale, multi-year dataset from a major Slovenian retailer. The dataset consists of 247,544 customers and 30 monthly time points, as well as the target month. To solve this regression problem, we construct a percustomer, multivariate monthly time series representing shopping habits, spending, visits, and related behavioral signals. We also enrich this data with static attributes of the customers. To model the shopping dynamics, including short-term shifts, medium-term cycles, and long-term seasonality, we propose a 1D Convolutional Neural Network that combines Inception-style multi-branch modules with Residual connections (IncRes). The Inception branches apply parallel convolutions with different kernel sizes and dilations to capture multi-scale temporal structure, while residual pathways stabilize optimization and support deeper, wider representations. Static features are integrated using a lightweight dense branch and fused later in the architecture, enabling the model to also learn behavior from the demographic context of the customers. The proposed model achieves an RRMSE of 0.679, outperforming the global mean baseline (1.165), a parameter-matched CNN model without Inception-Residual paradigms (0.720), as well as additional competing approaches including LSTM (0.717), GRU (0.714), MLP (0.715), Ridge (0.704), and ElasticNet (0.702). These results show that multi-scale convolutional representations with residual learning improve spend prediction accuracy from raw signals without manual feature engineering, providing a practical approach for individualized retail forecasting at scale, while achieving lower error rates against competing models.

title: Fast Distributed Stationary Vector Computation for quasi-Sparse Markov Chains
authors: Daniel Silvestre, João Hespanha, Carlos Silvestre
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: In this paper, we tackle the problem of computing the stationary distribution vector of a Markov Chain that is dense but whose structure has several entries equal to the same value. We first decompose the Markov matrix such that it exhibits a sparse structure (hence the quasi-sparse terminology) and provide its formulation as the solution of an optimization problem, a linear equation or the solution of an eigenvector problem. A result is presented that shows optimization algorithms to grow quadratically the problem condition number. The solution as an eigenvector problem using the Power iteration corresponds the Jacobi method when applied to the corresponding equation. Thus, we propose the use of the Gauss-Seidel or the Successive Over-Relaxation (SOR) methods to tackle the problem using synchronous and asynchronous communication that is shown to have better worst-case convergence rate. We illustrate through simulations for scale-free Markov Chains, based on the Barabási-Albert model, that the proposed algorithms achieve faster convergence in particular when subsequent solutions are required with Markov chains that are similar to the previous computation.

title: Identifying Critical Breakpoints and Latency Effects During Remote Backup Driving_supp1-3691492.pdf
authors: Anthony D. McDonald
publish: N/A
abstract: As Level 4 driving automation progresses, remote driving remains a critical fallback for commercial trucks operating at the edge of their operational design domains. However, network latency presents a major challenge, impairing driving performance, increasing cognitive workload, and reducing trust in the remote driving system. This study investigates how latency affects human factors and driving performance in remote driving scenarios through a within-subject driving simulator experiment. Results showed that latency significantly increased perceived workload and reduced trust, with no direct effect on fatigue or situation awareness. Driving performance was also affected: median speed decreased, steering corrections intensified, and steering entropy increased under higher latency. Segment analysis revealed that lane-keeping tasks—particularly in curved road segments—were most sensitive to latency, exhibiting the strongest degradation in control stability. In contrast, lane-changing maneuvers were less affected, likely due to their discrete and anticipatory nature. Segmented regression identified critical latency breakpoints (347–480 ms), beyond which trust in the remote driving system and driving performance exhibited substantial changes. These findings highlight the need for latency-aware, context-sensitive design strategies, including predictive assistance, and adaptive feedback interfaces, to support safe and efficient remote truck operation under fluctuating network conditions.

title: CodeAct: Codebook-guided Multi-skill Integration for Dynamic Humanoid Whole-body Control_supp2-3698369.mp4
authors: Ran Song
publish: N/A
abstract: Recent advances in humanoid robotics have showcased the potential of imitation learning (IL) to replicate dexterous whole-body behaviors in unstructured environments. However, conventional IL approaches that integrate multiple skills into a unified policy often suffer from training bias toward dominant or easily learned samples, resulting in a compromised action space that fails to preserve fine-grained dynamics of individual skills. To address this limitation, we propose CodeAct, a novel two-stage IL framework that facilitates multi-skill integration through structured expert code guidance. In the first stage, individual motions are distilled into high-fidelity expert codes, which are used to populate a dynamic codebook. In the second stage, a codebook-guided attention mechanism leverages these structured expert priors to constrain the optimization of the multi-skill policy. Importantly, expert codes serve as structured skill-specific representations, providing fine-grained supervision signals for different skills throughout multi-skill integration, thus helping reduce training bias and enabling seamless skill composition. Extensive experiments in simulation and on a real Unitree G1 humanoid robot demonstrate that CodeAct achieves competitive performance in both single-skill learning and multi-skill integration.

title: First-Order Friction Models With Bristle Dynamics: Lumped and Distributed Formulations
authors: Luigi Romano, Ole Morten Aamo, Jan Åslund, Erik Frisk
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control Systems Technology
abstract: Dynamic models, particularly rate-dependent models, have proven effective in capturing the key phenomenological features of frictional processes, whilst also possessing important mathematical properties that facilitate the design of control and estimation algorithms. However, many rate-dependent formulations are built on empirical considerations, whereas physical derivations may offer greater interpretability. In this context, starting from fundamental physical principles, this article introduces a novel class of first-order dynamic friction models that approximate the dynamics of a bristle element by inverting the friction characteristic. Amongst the developed models, a specific formulation closely resembling the LuGre model is derived using a simple rheological equation for the bristle element. This model is rigorously analyzed in terms of stability and passivity—important properties that support the synthesis of observers and controllers. Furthermore, a distributed version, formulated as a hyperbolic partial differential equation (PDE), is presented, which enables the modeling of frictional processes commonly encountered in rolling contact phenomena. The tribological behavior of the proposed description is evaluated through classical experiments and validated against the response predicted by the LuGre model, revealing both notable similarities and key differences. A simple but relevant control application is also illustrated.

title: Topology-Aware Integrated Communication, Sensing, and Power Transfer for Multi-User SAGIN
authors: Han Yu, Jiajun He, Xinping Yi, Feng Yin, Hing Cheung So, Giuseppe Caire
publish: IEEE Journal on Selected Areas in Communications
abstract: In sixth-generation and beyond, space-air-ground integrated networks (SAGINs) extend network connectivity to space, thereby enabling broader service coverage. This paper proposes a topology-aware SAGIN framework to address the integrated sensing, communication, and wireless power transfer (ISCPT) problem, leveraging the distinctive visibility of satellite-terrestrial and satellite-satellite users as well as their constructing in-between channel strengths. By modeling the topology of the SAGIN as a bipartite graph, we formulate the ISCPT problem as a multi-objective joint optimization problem with specified topological structures to reflect connection relationships of satellite-terrestrial and satellite-satellite users. The ISCPT problem is then reformulated and carefully decomposed as several mixed-integer linear programs (MILPs) by leveraging the network topology to individually optimize sensing, communication, and power transfer. To reduce the computational complexity of the proposed method, a greedy algorithm deal with generalized multi-assignment problem (GMAP) is developed. Simulation results demonstrate superior performance in communication and sensing, with a tolerable trade-off in wireless power transfer.

title: How Does Digital Platform Use Strengthen Organizational Resilience? The Role of Business Model Innovation and Entrepreneurial Orientation_supp1-3699417.docx
authors: Jiabao Lin
publish: N/A
abstract: While digital platform use is acknowledged to hold potential for boosting organizational resilience, the intrinsic mechanisms and contextual conditions shaping this relationship remain poorly understood. Drawing on organizational learning theory, this study investigates how digital platform use shapes organizational resilience, analyzing the mediating role of business model innovation (BMI) as well as the moderating effect of entrepreneurial orientation. Paired survey data from 178 agricultural firms is analyzed using partial least squares structural equation modeling and hierarchical ordinary least squares regression analysis. Results demonstrate that digital platform use significantly enhances BMI, which subsequently fosters proactive resilience and reactive resilience. BMI acts as a full mediator in the association of digital platform use with both proactive resilience and reactive resilience. While entrepreneurial orientation fails to moderate the association of BMI with reactive resilience, it significantly reinforces the association of BMI with proactive resilience, thus amplifying the mediating role of BMI in the link between digital platform use and proactive resilience. This study advances existing literature by providing theoretical elaborations and empirical validation on how digital platform use enables organizational resilience, and provides implications for enterprises to use digital platforms for the sake of developing organizational resilience.

title: Vector-Space Global Collocation Method for Modeling Rod-Driven Parallel Continuum Manipulators_supp1-3694012.mp4
authors: Rui Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Parallel continuum manipulators (PCMs) have attracted increasing attention owing to their flexibility, high precision, and strong load-bearing capacity, compared with their single-arm continuum counterparts. In this article, a kinetostatic modeling framework for PCMs is developed based on Cosserat rod theory, and a vector-space global collocation method (GCM) is proposed to solve the derived model. The GCM selects appropriate basis functions to approximate the unknown functions in vector space under different loading and actuation conditions. In particular, variables originally defined on the Lie group, such as rotation matrices, are reformulated entirely in the vector space while preserving orthogonality, thereby avoiding complex mathematical and numerical operations on the manifold. The proposed method is validated through FEM and experimental tests on a 3-rod PCM prototype. The maximum relative error in the rotation angle is 2.69%, and the absolute error in the end-effector height variation is 1.1 mm, corresponding to 0.55% of its initial height. Finally, a conceptual flexible vector thruster is demonstrated, within which a PCM-based thruster serves as a core component. The kinetostatic and compliance characteristics of this PCM-based thruster are analyzed using the proposed GCM-based semianalytical framework highlighting the simplicity, efficiency, and robustness of the developed framework.

title: Hardware-Accelerated Parallel Wrong-Path Execution for Spectre Gadget Detection
authors: Fangzheng Lin, Zeyu Gao, Yanru Lin, Yangyu Chen, Hiroshi Sasaki
publish: IEEE Computer Architecture Letters
abstract: Spectre vulnerabilities pose new threats to computer system security, and detecting Spectre gadgets is an important step toward effective countermeasures. Detection requires exploring program behavior along branch-mispredicted execution paths (i.e., wrong paths), which existing dynamic scanners emulate in software—incurring high overhead and limiting parallel exploration. This paper proposes a hardware-assisted dynamic gadget detection architecture that offloads wrong-path executions to a pool of in-order shadow cores on a specialized asymmetric multicore platform, enabling multiple paths to be explored in parallel. The design also features shadow memory versioning to preserve the memory view at the branch point without full memory duplication. We prototype the proposed architecture on an Xilinx VU13P FPGA. Evaluation shows that the prototype achieves 1.25–5.24× speedup over software-based detectors.

title: Span-Level Distributed Polarization Dependent Loss Measurement: A Longitudinal Power Monitoring Approach
authors: Peiyun Ge, Tianrun Sun, Yichao Wang, Lixia Xi, Xiaoguang Zhang
publish: Journal of Lightwave Technology
abstract: Polarization dependent loss (PDL) is a major impairment limiting the performance of dual-polarization coherent optical systems, which is primarily introduced by reconfigurable optical add-drop multiplexers (ROADMs) and other optical devices along fiber links. It induces signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) imbalance and fluctuation between the two polarizations and critically degrades the quality of transmission (QoT). In this paper, we propose a span-level distributed PDL measurement scheme based on the longitudinal power monitoring (LPM) approach. The proposed scheme establishes a sequential state of polarization (SOP) sweeping framework, in which local PDL estimation is performed step-by-step by recursively loading previously estimated PDL responses onto the reference signal, thereby aligning the estimation basis with each local PDL element rather than with the equivalent PDL acquired by a single global SOP-sweeping strategy. Benefiting from the advantages of LPM, the proposed method enables fully distributed local PDL measurement in the receiver-side digital signal processing (DSP) domain, without pilot tones (PTs) overhead, while maintaining flexible and costeffective operation. Extensive numerical simulations validate the theoretical analysis and demonstrate a mean estimation error of -0.11 dB over a single-channel 140-GBaud 8 × 80 km single mode fiber (SMF) link, with almost all relative errors within <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pm$</tex-math></inline-formula> 20%, while remaining computationally efficient.

title: A Novel Remez-Like Algorithm for Accurate Synthesis of High-Order Multiband Filters
authors: Ping Zhao, Jiyuan Fan, Jinzhu Zhou, Ke Wu
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: Remez-like algorithms are commonly used to construct multiband filter characteristics for which closed-form solutions are unavailable. Existing Remez-like algorithms expand the unknown numerator polynomial as a power series of the frequency variable. This formulation introduces Vandermonde matrices into the interpolation equations, leading to numerical instability in high-order filter synthesis. To overcome this limitation, this work proposes a novel Remez-like algorithm based on the partial fraction expansion of the rational reflection characteristic. The proposed technique enhances numerical stability and enables accurate synthesis of high-order multiband filters, as demonstrated by examples.

title: Convergence Behavior of Alternated-Constrained Partitioned-Block Frequency-Domain Adaptive Filters
authors: Zhengqiang Luo, Weiqiang Tan, Feiran Yang
publish: IEEE Signal Processing Letters
abstract: The alternated-constrained partitioned-block frequency-domain adaptive filter (AC-PBFDAF) provides an effective balance between convergence performance and computational efficiency. This letter examines the convergence characteristics of the AC-PBFDAF based on the theory of difference equations with periodic coefficients. We first study the evolution of the mean weight vector and show that the AC PBFDAF converges to the true unknown impulse response. Using vectorization operation, we derive the recursive formulas of the mean-square deviation (MSD). We then establish conditions for mean-square convergence and obtain closed-form expressions for the steady-state MSD. The analysis shows that the steady-state MSD exhibit periodic fluctuation, and the fluctuation period is determined by the partition number and constraint period. Monte Carlo experiments verify the analytical predictions with excellent consistency.

title: Towards Lightweight CKKS: On Client Cost Efficiency
authors: Jung Hee Cheon, Minsik Kang, Jai Hyun Park
publish: Proceedings of the ACM Asia Conference on Computer and Communications Security

title: Efficiency Improvements for Signal's Handshake Protocol
authors: Barbara Jiabao Benedikt, Sebastian Clermont, Marc Fischlin, Tobias Schmalz

title: PUE Schemes: Efficient Updatable Encryption With Robust Security From Symmetric Primitives
authors: Andreas Weninger, Elena Andreeva

title: POSTER: FuzzyExtract - A Lightweight Approach for Cryptographic Bill of Materials Extraction from Java Binaries
authors: Manish Shukla, Sachin Lodha
