title: 3D Porous Media Reconstruction via a Hybrid Diffusion Model and Generative Adversarial Network
authors: Hongwei Yang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Porous media reconstruction is essential for characterizing internal structures and physical properties. However, accurate three-dimensional reconstruction under limited data remains challenging due to structural complexity and stochasticity. Existing generative adversarial network (GAN) methods often lack diversity in high-dimensional tasks, while diffusion models suffer from high sampling costs. To address these issues, this paper proposes a hybrid GAN–diffusion framework for 3D porous media reconstruction. The method reformulates the denoising process via reparameterization and leverages GAN to learn multimodal distributions, improving efficiency while preserving statistical consistency. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves good reconstruction quality and computational efficiency.

title: Pipeline Leakage Signal Recognition via LMD-Guided Local-Attention Transformer
authors: Yu Luo, Chang Liu, Yufei Wang, Guangfu Ren
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Recognizing pipeline leakage signals in field environments is difficult because leakage-induced infrasonic responses are weak, non-stationary, and easily submerged by background interference. This paper presents a task-oriented leakage recognition framework that couples Local Mean Decomposition (LMD) with a local-attention Transformer. Rather than loosely cascading two existing modules, the method is co-designed for low-SNR leakage signals: LMD with a correlation coefficient-kurtosis joint screening criterion suppresses noise-dominated PF components and preserves informative transient leakage components, while sliding-window tokenization, neighborhood-constrained local attention, and lightweight relative positional encoding jointly capture local leakage mutations and longer-range temporal dependencies at lower attention cost. Experiments on measured field data from above-ground and buried pipeline sections show 97.15% average accuracy and 0.46% false alarm rate, outperforming the compared baselines. Under the current GPU setting, the proposed method requires 24 ms per batch of 64 samples, which is lower than the standard Transformer. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed task-specific signal-enhancement and sequence-modeling design for leakage recognition in noisy field conditions.

title: A Fast Consensus Algorithm for Multi-Agent Systems Based on Motion Inertia and Density Perception
authors: Chuangtao Zhong
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Addressing the challenges of slow convergence, high communication costs, and neglect of motion inertia in large-scale distributed multi-agent systems under mixed topologies, this paper proposes a fast consensus algorithm based on extreme neighbor selection and density perception. The algorithm interacts with boundary neighbors possessing the extreme horizontal and vertical coordinates within the local communication radius to compute an overall optimal vector, drastically reducing communication overhead. Concurrently, the DBSCAN clustering algorithm is introduced for local density perception, enabling an adaptive switching logic: the historical movement inertia vector is activated to accelerate convergence in non-crowded states, while inertia is suppressed to ensure system stability and prevent collisions in crowded states. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method significantly enhances state convergence speed while substantially reducing the total number of communication connections, offering an efficient and robust solution for large-scale, high-density dynamic multi-agent cooperative control.

title: A Perceived-Discrepancy-Based Method for Evaluating Automotive Seat Comfort
authors: Jiakuo Ni
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Perception and Autonomous Control (IPAC)
abstract: With the rapid development of the automotive industry, cockpit comfort—especially seat comfort—has become a key determinant of driving experience. However, conventional seat evaluation still suffers from (i) a weak alignment between subjective feelings and objective indicators, (ii) strong individual differences, and (iii) insufficient coverage of real driving scenes. To address these issues, this study proposes a perceived-discrepancy- based automotive seat comfort evaluation method. An expectation-disconfirmation-driven dissatisfaction model is constructed, where the dissatisfaction indicator for each criterion is defined as <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N_s = max((I_s - S_s)/I_s, 0)$</tex>, and the overall dissatisfaction is obtained by importance-normalized weights <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$w_s =I \_\mathrm{s} / \Sigma I \_\mathrm{s}$</tex> and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$D=\Sigma w_s.N_s$</tex>. We validate the method through a real-vehicle experiment and an owner survey on a Tesla Model Y using a 15-indicator multidimensional system. Results show that the largest dissatisfaction occurs in vibration comfort (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{N} {+} \mathbf{s} {=} \mathbf{0. 1 8 2}$</tex>; 184 complaints) and lumbar support curvature (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$N \_s=0.167; 170$</tex> complaints), followed by seatbelt-related vibration transmission (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N} \_\mathrm{s}=0.098$</tex>; 157 complaints) and seat hardness (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathrm{N} \_\mathrm{s}=0.093$</tex>; 144 complaints). Moreover, the model-based dissatisfaction indicator is strongly correlated with the observed dissatisfaction instances (Spearman <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\rho=0.886, \mathrm{p}<0.001$</tex>), demonstrating that perceived discrepancy can reliably identify seat deficiencies with high interpretability. These findings provide actionable guidance for intelligent seat design optimization and multi-criteria comfort benchmarking in real driving scenarios.

title: HyFi-Fake: Hybrid Deep Learning for High-Fidelity Deepfake Generation and Detection
authors: Suchir Kaushik, Kamal Kumar Gola, Priyank Tyagi, Aafia Hussain, Arti Pandey, Hriday Kumar Gupta
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: This study introduces a hybrid framework for deepfake creation and detection that integrates CNN, RNN, LSTM, YOLOv12, GAN, and Transformer architectures. A 10 GB dataset of segmented facial features was used for training (1000 epochs per parameter). The model generated highly realistic and temporally consistent outputs, achieving 98.2% accuracy, an FID score of 6.2, an SSIM of 0.96, and a PSNR of 38.4 dB. Furthermore, 92 % of the deepfakes were incorrectly recognized as authentic by human evaluators, and the generated content was able to bypass advanced detection models such as ViT, XceptionNet, and CapsuleNet.

title: A Comparative Study of Path Planning and Trajectory Tracking Algorithms for Mobile Robots
authors: Haoyi Liu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: With the rapid development of autonomous driving and mobile robots, collaborative optimization of path planning and trajectory tracking has become essential for autonomous navigation. This paper investigates two typical path-planning algorithms, Dijkstra and A*, and two common trajectory-tracking approaches, PID and Pure Pursuit. Experiments are conducted in complex maze and open terrain environments, with evaluation metrics including planning efficiency, path curvature, tracking accuracy, and steering stability. Parameter sensitivity analyses are performed for the look-ahead distance in Pure Pursuit and the proportional coefficient in PID. Experimental results indicate that both Dijkstra and A* can obtain optimal paths. A* exhibits higher planning efficiency in open environments, while the two algorithms perform similarly in complex scenarios. A* generally generates paths with higher curvature. For trajectory tracking, PID achieves better tracking accuracy due to closed-loop correction, but tends to cause more frequent steering adjustments. In contrast, Pure Pursuit provides smoother motion and more stable control, though with slightly lower precision. The look-ahead distance of Pure Pursuit affects the balance between accuracy and smoothness, while the proportional gain of PID mainly influences steering stability rather than tracking accuracy. This study reveals the adaptive characteristics of planning and tracking algorithms under different environmental conditions and parameter settings. It provides valuable references for algorithm selection and parameter tuning in autonomous navigation systems for mobile robots.

title: Joint Iterative Deconvolution of GC-MS Data Using Non-Negative Matrix Factorization and Exponentially Modified Gaussian Constraints
authors: Biao Liu, Yaotong Li, Bingrong Dai
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Severe co-elution in gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC-MS) often masks trace components and distorts spectral matching. To resolve this, we propose a joint iterative optimization framework that combines physical chromatographic constraints with matrix factorization. The process begins with dynamic m/z binning and non-negative matrix factorization (NMF) to establish initial elution and mass spectral bases. We then introduce an exponentially modified Gaussian (EMG) model and apply an alternating optimization strategy: updating the mass spectra via non-negative least squares (NNLS) and fine-tuning EMG parameters via non-linear least squares. To avoid subjective guessing, an adaptive penalty mechanism automatically determines the optimal number of components. Tested on a public metabolomics dataset, our framework completely avoids the multi-peak splitting seen in data-driven models and the signal cross-talk common in MCR-ALS. The method reliably extracts trace components, preserves their true asymmetric tailing, and achieves a global explained variance above 99%.

title: Secure and Decentralized Voting System Leveraging Blockchain and Edge-Fog Architecture
authors: Tarun Kumar, Tanu, Kadugula Joel, Ashish Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: Electronic voting systems have increased both elec- toral efficiency and accessibility, electronic voting results con- tinue to face significant security, transparency, scalability, and anonymity-related challenges. In addition, the ability of all e- voting infrastructures to have a single point of failure reinforces the need for public verifiability; blockchain-based voting systems provide resistance to tampering, however, they often encounter issues with heavy latency, limited scalability, and high computational overhead associated with large-scale elections. This paper proposes a “Secure and Decentralized Voting System Leveraging Blockchain and Edge-Fog Architecture as a solution to the problems just described. This approach to e-voting uses the processing power of edge technology for secure verification of the voter's identity, the use of fog technology for intermediate confirmation of the vote and distribution of workload to different locations, and blockchain to create an immutable vote storage and provide end-to-end verification of the vote. By combining the processing power of edge and fog technology, this system is expected to significantly reduce the amount of traffic on the blockchain as well as the amount of time it takes to confirm votes while still maintaining voter anonymity and the integrity of the election process.

title:
Amplitude Modulation and Phase Modulation Based on CA
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    RC Algorithm in DFRC
authors: Yuqi Liang, Hongyuan Hu, Zehui Wu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: By sharing the same hardware platform, spectrum resources, and transmit waveforms, dual-functional radar-communication (DFRC) systems are envisioned as a key technology for future wireless networks. In this paper, we employ the complex accurate array response control (CA<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>RC) algorithm to precisely adjust the received magnitude and phase response of communication direction, realizing amplitude modulation and phase modulation for DFRC systems. For phase modulation, the quadrature phase shift keying (QPSK) scheme is adopted, where the transmitter emits two orthogonal waveforms, and the phase difference between them carries the phase information to be transmitted. Moreover, the sidelobe level in the communication direction is controlled to realize amplitude modulation. Numerical and analytical results demonstrate that the proposed method enables integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems to achieve magnitude and phase modulation with the CA<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>RC algorithm reducing computational complexity from cubic to linear order.

title: Integrating Residual and Spatial Attention in Invertible Networks for Image Steganography
authors: Zhijun Yuan, Dongliang Li, Wenzhong Yang, Shiji Sun
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Image steganography seeks to conceal confidential data in an unnoticeable way through cover images for covert transmission. However, most existing methods are unable to maintain good visual effects or robust performance for Images containing Complex Textures. In light of these deficiencies, we have introduced the Residual-Spatial Attention Invertible Neural Network (RSAINN) to integrate robust feature representation and attended-learning. It can perceive the faint details on objects and among other different levels of information, thereby adjusting its focusing point as needed. Residual-Spatial Attention Module fuses multi-level low-level features hierarchically through hierarchical skip connections, improving feature fusion and avoiding severe visual distortions for enhancing stego-image security. Also improve the refined network structure through modifications to convolutional layers or other means; deepen their application to enhance feature refinement capabilities. Based on experimental results for DIV2K, COCO and ImageNet, we have shown that our method can significantly improve the stego-image Quality; The increase in PSNR is 0.35dB, 6.61dB and 4.8db than baselines respectively.

title: A Safety Helmet Wearing Detection Method Based on Lightweight Multi-Scale Attention and Maturity Awareness
authors: Taiji Pan, Jianghai Li, Qiang Li
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: The detection of safety helmet wearing on construction sites is a crucial measure for preventing accidents caused by falls from height and being struck by objects. To address the issues of large parameter sizes in existing detection models and their insufficient focus on critical areas, this paper proposes a lightweight, multi-scale attention module named MaturityAwareHDPA. This module captures global contextual information through parallel multi-scale average pooling and introduces a learnable maturity weight matrix. It dynamically modulates channel attention based on the wearing status (worn/not worn), achieving adaptive feature enhancement for samples in different states. By embedding this module into the YOLOv11 backbone network, experimental results on a self-constructed dataset demonstrate that the parameter count is reduced by 63%, and the overall detection accuracy (mAP50) is improved by 1.5%, with the detection accuracy for the helmet specifically increasing by 2.4%. This method enhances detection performance while significantly compressing the model size, offering an effective solution for real-time safety monitoring in resource-constrained scenarios.

title: Research on the Construction Method of Vocational Education Training Platform Combining Digital Twinning and Reinforcement Learning
authors: Qin Juan, Li Xingchen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Low-Altitude Airspace and Artificial Intelligence (LAAI)
abstract: This paper proposes a method for constructing a vocational education training platform that integrates Digital Twin (DT) and Reinforcement Learning (RL). The platform adopts a layered architecture comprising a “DT virtual layer—RL agent layer—user interaction layer.” It utilizes Unity3D to construct a high-fidelity virtual environment, achieving bidirectional mapping with real equipment. By introducing a hierarchical state space model and action discretization mechanism, it reduces the learning difficulty of complex vocational skill training. A multi-objective reward function is designed to simultaneously optimize operational compliance, efficiency, and safety. Empirical research demonstrates that in industrial robot sorting tasks, experimental group students significantly outperformed traditional training models (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{p}<0.01$</tex>) across task completion time, operational compliance scores, accident rates, and adaptability to new tasks. This platform effectively enhances training efficiency and quality, cultivates students' comprehensive vocational competencies aligned with smart manufacturing demands, and provides a reusable technical pathway for vocational education's digital transformation.

title: Research on Pedestrian Trajectory Prediction Based on Density Perception and Causal Gated Spatiotemporal Graph Network
authors: Jie Wu, Chaozhu Zhang, Yanan Zhang, Fangyu Li, Maohai Lin
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Pedestrian trajectory prediction is crucial in fields such as autonomous driving. Addressing the limitations of existing methods—specifically their fixed weights for spatio-temporal feature fusion and their failure to account for crowd density and actual collision risks when modeling social interactions—this paper proposes a Spatio-Temporal Graph Convolutional Network based on scene density awareness and social causal gating. The proposed model dynamically assesses crowd density via a scene density estimator; introduces a social causal gating unit, based on Time-to-Collision (TTC), to filter out risk-free and redundant interactions; and designs an adaptive fusion mechanism to dynamically adjust the weights of spatio-temporal features according to density. Experiments conducted on the ETH and UCY datasets demonstrate that the proposed method significantly reduces both the Average Displacement Error (ADE) and the Final Displacement Error (FDE), exhibiting superior robustness in complex interaction scenarios.

title: Research on Mobile Game User Segmentation Based on Hybrid Clustering Model
authors: Jingying Fu, Fusheng Chen, Chiawei Chu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Aiming at the high-dimensional, sparse, and right-skewed distribution characteristics of game user behavior data, this study proposes a hybrid clustering algorithm fusing Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise (DBSCAN) and K-Means++. First, Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) was performed on 829,000 game user behavior records to verify the "whale effect" in payment behavior, followed by the elimination of outliers via the three-sigma (3σ) rule. A three-dimensional feature system was then constructed, and seven principal components accounting for 83.2% of the total variance were retained after variance filtering, correlation analysis, and Principal Component Analysis (PCA) dimensionality reduction. Optuna-based Bayesian Optimization (BO) was adopted to jointly tune the key parameters of DBSCAN (eps and min_samples) and K-Means++ (k value), yielding the optimal parameter combination: eps=0.525, min_samples=46, and k=5. Experimental results show that the proposed hybrid model outperforms the single K-Means and DBSCAN baseline models in all three key clustering evaluation metrics, with a Silhouette Coefficient (SC) of 0.6304, a Calinski-Harabasz (CH) index of 265,163.72, and a Davies-Bouldin (DB) index of 0.6912. Further business analysis identifies three core user segments (high-value whale users, potential users, and price-sensitive users), thereby offering data-driven insights for precision user operation in the gaming industry.

title: Quantifying Audio-Visual Fusion Gains Under Fixed-Feature Constraints on YouTube-8M
authors: Bohong Huang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: While scene understanding in unconstrained videos is inherently multimodal, the practical benefit of adding audio to visual models remains unclear under long-tail distributions. This paper presents an empirical study on the YouTube-8M dataset, focusing on 25-class scene classification using video-level precomputed features (1024D RGB and 128D audio). We compare five model settings: Audio-only, RGB-only, early-fusion, gated fusion, and cross-attention fusion. Results show visual features dominate (micro-F1: 0.84 vs. 0.65), but multimodal fusion provides a consistent gain (micro-F1: 0.85; macro-F1: 0.70). Category-level analysis reveals a "distributed complementarity", with 18 of 25 classes exhibiting distinct improvements from audio fusion. Through controlled binary-class experiments, we observe that the audio performance ceiling is heavily influenced by the semantic ambiguity of the 128D representation rather than classifier limitations. To address the extreme 321:1 class imbalance, we evaluate mitigation strategies, finding complex loss reweighting unstable in this fixed-feature setup, whereas post-processing per-class threshold optimization effectively lifts macro-F1 from 0.32 to 0.36. This study offers reproducible baseline guidance for deploying multimodal systems under fixed-feature constraints.

title: Hardware Acceleration Research on Lossless Image Compression Algorithm Based on FPGA for CCSDS
authors: Sen Yang, Huajun Shi
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: In the current aerospace industry, large-scale image data acquired by onboard equipment impose significant storage and transmission challenges. Given limited onboard resources, there is an urgent need for efficient lossless image compression methods to address these challenges. This study implements hardware acceleration and optimization of the CCSDS(Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems)-standard DPAM(Differential Pulse Code Modulation)+Rice lossless image compression algorithm on an FPGA platform. To overcome the slow performance of traditional software implementations, we propose an efficient hardware acceleration scheme that employs a chunking method in the Rice encoding module—dividing data into groups of 32 units and determining the K-value based on intra-group maxima, thus substantially reducing computational complexity for K-value selection. To enhance processing speed, a ping-pong dual-buffering mechanism is introduced in the Rice encoding module to enable pipeline processing. Additionally, a multi-take encoding approach is adopted to mitigate timing issues during high-speed operation. Simulation using ModelSim and FPGA board testing demonstrate that the designed compression module achieves a maximum operating clock frequency of 100MHz for the first time, single-module compression rates of 32MB/s to 40MB/s, compression ratios exceeding 1.5, and complete lossless data recovery after compression. Compared to traditional software implementations, the hardware acceleration solution delivers significant speed improvements, fully meeting the rapid compression requirements of onboard equipment for massive image datasets, and demonstrating substantial application value in space data systems.

title: Coherence-Minimized Sensing Matrix Design for MRI Reconstruction via Dual-Space Projection Optimization
authors: Siyuan Feng
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Compressed sensing magnetic resonance imaging (CS-MRI) heavily relies on the low mutual coherence between the measurement matrix and the sparsity basis. However, under highly accelerated Cartesian undersampling, the severe structural coherence between Fourier measurements and spatial bases, discrete cosine transform (DCT) for example, fundamentally violates this requirement, causing classical sparse recovery algorithms to stagnate. To mitigate this fundamental bottleneck, we propose a synergistic dual-space projection framework, denoted as PAQ. Instead of merely designing heuristic sampling masks, our method directly reshapes the equivalent dictionary. Specifically, we introduce a diagonal-dominant random rotator Q in the feature space to probabilistically disrupt structural alignment, and an active orthogonalization projector P in the measurement space to deterministically whiten the residual correlations. We theoretically demonstrate that this dual-space mechanism bounds the mutual coherence with an exponentially decaying tail via sub-exponential distribution properties. Experimental validations on clinical MRI datasets under 20% Cartesian sampling demonstrate that plugging the PAQ preconditioners into the standard ISTA solver significantly suppresses aliasing artifacts and yields consistent peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR) improvements.

title: Research on the Dynamic Prediction Model of Personal Risk in Power Operations Based on Multi-Source Key Variables and an Improved BP Neural Network
authors: Xiao Yun, Dingfan He, Yang Yang, Haoran Zhou, Siqi Yang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: The working environment in the power industry is complex and ever-changing, and the personal risks involved are characterized by significant dynamism, coupling, and uncertainty. Traditional risk management mechanisms based on static assessments have obvious lags and are difficult to achieve real-time early warning of personal risks. To address this pain point, this paper proposes a dynamic prediction model for personal risks in power operations based on multi-source key variable extraction and an improved BP neural network. First, this paper delves into the evolution mechanism of power operation risks. Through text disambiguation (WSD) and knowledge graph technology, it cleans massive amounts of multi-source heterogeneous data and constructs a "multi-source element full-domain risk factor library" covering five dimensions: equipment, organization, individual, environment, and management, containing 54 risk factors. Secondly, the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) was introduced to quantitatively screen the initial 54 global risk factors, which were then reduced to 25 core secondary indicators. Hierarchical analysis and weight calculation were performed on these 25 secondary indicators to finally establish the key variable system driving the prediction model. Subsequently, to overcome the shortcomings of traditional BP neural networks, such as being prone to getting trapped in local optima and having slow convergence speed, this paper introduces genetic algorithm (GA), particle swarm optimization (PSO), and fruit fly optimization algorithm (FOA) to globally optimize the weights and thresholds of the network. Simulation verification using 524 sets of field measurement data shows that the models optimized by the three algorithms are superior to the traditional models in terms of convergence speed and prediction accuracy. Among them, the model optimized by the Fruit Fly Optimization (FOA) algorithm is the most outstanding, with not only a faster convergence speed but also the best prediction accuracy, providing scientific engineering application support for on-site warning and dynamic early warning of personal risks in power plants.

title: A Domain-Adapted Multi-Source Ensemble Framework for Real-Time Crop Recommendation in Indian Agro-Zones
authors: Parvatini Manideep, Andol Saketh, Vishwanadula Venu Madhav Chary, Vanamala Durga Prasad, Gumma Nagarjuna, Seema Rao, Omdev Dahiya
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: This paper introduces an integrated crop recommendation framework that combines multi-source agricultural data, domain adaptation, and real-time environmental intelligence. The system utilizes two visually and statistically different datasets, Kaggle's crop recommendation data and a region-specific Indian dataset, and aligns their feature distributions by applying Gaussian noise-based adaptation. Five heterogeneous models (Random Forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, and TabNet) are trained independently, and their class probability outputs are aggregated through a stacked meta-learning classifier for improved stability and predictive strength. To account for regional soil variability, a nutrient fusion mechanism incorporates state-level Soil Health Card (SHC) statistics with user-provided NPK and pH inputs. Live temperature, humidity, and rainfall values obtained from the Open Weather API further refine predictions by incorporating current field conditions. TabNet's attentive feature-selection mechanism enhances interpretability, while the ensemble consistently outperforms individual models across multiple evaluation scenarios. The proposed approach demonstrates strong generalization across Indian agricultural zones, providing a practical decisionsupport tool that helps farmers choose optimal crops and enhance yield outcomes.

title: X GENESIS: A Hybrid Blockchain Framework for Secure and Scalable Credential Verification
authors: Ramneet Singh, Palkin Maan, Mridul Aggarwal, Raghav Gupta, Achintya Pandey
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The problem of verifying the authenticity of academic and professional credentials has been one of the biggest challenges. The conventional methods such as resumes, certificates, and online profiles can be easily faked, and there is no trusted system for their verification. Even though blockchain with its transparency and immutability seems an attractive solution, but implementing it on a large scale is very expensive, too slow, and even complicated. We introduce X GENESIS, a hybrid framework that integrates blockchain with AI, to solve these problems. X GENESIS, through Layer-2 scaling, batch NFT minting, off-chain storage to store the metadata and hash on chain (Commit-Store Pattern) with event driven architecture, enables credential issuance to be done in a cheaper cost and more efficient manner. So we have build a Decentralized application which mints the SBT's (Soulbound tokens) and NFT's (Non- Fungible token) as per the usage and type of credential. Our main focus is on academic credentials, so here the users can hold NFT's and can list them to others. Our application also aim that real talent should be valued and also for the recruiters perspective they can get the real talent they want without wasting there time on the false profiles. So to make our application more secure we have build AI agents, which checks for plagiarism, fraud detection, skill extraction, and recommendation insight provision, so trust is further enhanced. As a result <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{X}$</tex> GENESIS can achieve higher throughput and lower operational costs than traditional blockchain methods existing. This framework ensures that credentials are verifiable, tamper-proof, and built to last, which can be used in universities, hiring platforms, and government bodies that issue official certifications.

title: Research on the Application of Reinforcement Learning Algorithms in Multi-Objective Scheduling of Construction Engineering
authors: Longkun Lu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Low-Altitude Airspace and Artificial Intelligence (LAAI)
abstract: This paper mainly studies the role of reinforcement learning algorithms in multi-objective scheduling of construction projects. By using the scheduling model of reinforcement learning algorithms, it aims to solve multi-objective problems such as construction period, cost, quality and safety in construction projects. Through techniques such as state discretization, action constraints, and reward function design, reinforcement learning algorithms can adapt to many factors such as the complexity and constraints of engineering scheduling in multi-objective scheduling. Experimental data show that reinforcement learning algorithms have strong flexibility in optimizing task scheduling, resource allocation, cost control, and safety management, etc. It also provides a reference idea for the application research of reinforcement learning algorithms in construction engineering.

title: A Hybrid PSO-SSA Algorithm Synergized with Pattern Search for 3D UAV Path Planning
authors: Xiang Liu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Addressing the complex three-dimensional trajectory planning problem for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs), this paper proposes a novel hybrid meta-heuristic framework integrating Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) and the Sparrow Search Algorithm (SSA), further synergized with Pattern Search (PS). To balance global exploration and local exploitation, a cyclic phase-shifting mechanism with a strict 20-iteration alternation step is designed. Furthermore, an adaptive density sampling method based on exact spline derivatives is introduced to rigorously evaluate kinematic curvature limits. By employing exponential collision penalty functions and coordinate sorting regularization, kinematically feasible and collision-free B-spline trajectories are ensured. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed framework outclasses conventional algorithms in spatial accuracy, robustness, and convergence efficiency in constrained 3D mountainous topographies.

title: A Comprehensive Study on Evaluation Methods of Large Language Models
authors: Harshita Suri, Mukesh Kumar Bhardwaj, Bipin Pandey, Deepti Kulshrestha, Sourav Kumar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Systems in Engineering, Secured Systems and Cybersecurity (ICISESSC)
abstract: The rapid usage of large language models has made it very important to evaluate their performance, safety, credibility, and dependability. To be able to trust a model with our information, safety checks need to be done. The evaluation techniques that are currently being used for analyzing LLM outputs are discussed and examined in this paper. These evaluation techniques include a number of toxicity detection frameworks, bias analysis techniques, linguistic metrics like BLEU, semantic similarity models like Sentence-BERT, and hallucination detection systems. The limitations of the current methods and evaluation techniques make it inefficient to work with large language models. Our paper provides an integrated assessment tool that includes semantic and linguistic safety and fairness elements in order to evaluate the LLM'S responses accurately by checking the LLM outputs against multiple parameters. Our evaluation approach assesses the model's performance based on multiple factors like bias, hallucination, coherence, semantic similarity and toxicity which makes it dependable for enterprises as well as local applications. Our engine incorporates TF-IDF weighted Jaccard similarity, ROUGE-1 F1, exponential length fit, convex toxicity and bias penalties, coherence blending, hallucination risk calibration, and an overall safety-gated score. Our system includes a Chrome extension and Gradio dashboard, which enables real-time evaluation of chatbot responses with transparency and reproducibility. The validation is done through numerical examples and a 14-test suite is used for normalised outputs, convex penalty behaviour and effective safety gating.

title: Research on Precise Path Planning and Flight Control Methods for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles in Strong Electromagnetic Gis Environment
authors: Ke Du, Haoze Zhuo, Yani Nong
publish: 2025 International Conference on Low-Altitude Airspace and Artificial Intelligence (LAAI)
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles operate in highly complex strong electromagnetic (GIS) environments, and there are problems such as high failure rate and unstable control in their navigation routes and flight control. To enhance the autonomous operation capability of unmanned aerial vehicles, this paper adopts the electromagnetic interference model of geographic information System (GIS), integrates computer vision and multi-source sensor data, and generates a dynamic path cost map. Meanwhile, the deep reinforcement learning D3QN algorithm is adopted to improve the airway planning strategy, effectively avoiding interference sources. An anti-interference flight control architecture based on ROS is established from the perspective of flight control, and command-level adaptive adjustment is carried out by integrating real-time perception information.

title: Identification of Underwater Ship Radiated Noise Based on the Renyi Complexity-Energy Weighted Slope Entropy Plane
authors: Zhipeng Xu, Zhe Chen, Zhihan Wu, Caian Wei
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Feature extraction from underwater acoustic signals is a critical component of underwater target recognition. However, the complex marine ambient noise causes these signals to exhibit strong nonlinear, non-stationary, and non-Gaussian characteristics. Traditional methods, such as LOFAR spectrograms and MFCC, often suffer from low robustness and limited feature representation in such environments. Although entropy-based methods derived from chaos theory—such as Permutation Entropy (PE), Dispersion Entropy (DE), and Slope Entropy (SlEn)—can quantify the randomness of time series, they often fail to capture the underlying nonlinear structural information, which is critical for target discrimination. Furthermore, SlEn focuses solely on the differences between adjacent elements, neglecting the original amplitude information and making the selection of threshold parameters highly challenging. To address these limitations, this paper proposes an Energy Weighted Slope Entropy (EWSlEn) algorithm. Building upon this, we introduce a Renyi Complexity-Energy Weighted Slope Entropy (RC-EWSlEn) plane, which comprehensively measures both the randomness and the nonlinear structural information of acoustic sequences. Additionally, a threshold selection method based on the median and median absolute deviation (MAD) is proposed. This method provides a data-driven and physically interpretable baseline that significantly simplifies subsequent threshold optimization while offering improved robustness to extreme values compared to mean-variance approaches. Experimental results on the ShipsEar dataset demonstrate that when combined with a LightGBM classifier, the method achieves an average classification accuracy of 88.27% across the four target classes.

title: Passages
authors: Alex Groce
publish: ACM SIGSOFT Software Engineering Notes
abstract: None

title: Graph-Based Financial Fraud Detection with Calibrated Risk Scoring and Structural Regularization
authors: Yunfei Nie, Jiawei Wang, Ruobing Yan, Yuhan Wang, Zouxiaowei Ma, Yilun Wu
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 International Conference on AI Decision-Making and Management
abstract: None

title: Cloud-Edge Collaborative Zero Trust Access Control: Architecture and Scheme Design_supp1-3692784.pdf
authors: Lingyang Song
publish: N/A
abstract: Cloud-edge collaborative manufacturing systems have drawn considerable attention by offloading data processing tasks to edge servers to support latency-sensitive tasks and high manufacturing efficiency. In these systems, data can be accessed by external devices and the access connections are exposed on the Internet, leading to a risk of data leakage or interception. In this paper, to address such data security issues, we introduce the zero trust architecture (ZTA) where every user, whether internal or external, is required to be authorized and verified before accessing any resource, thereby ensuring data security. However, existing ZTA implementations primarily leverage security technologies that rely on either biometrics and human behavior or sparse device attributes, rendering them inapplicable in cloud-edge collaborative manufacturing systems. To deal with this challenge, we propose a cloud-edge collaborative ZTA (CE-ZTA), in which ciphertext-policy attribute-based encryption (CP-ABE) is employed to implement ZTA principles. We propose a continuously-verifying CP-ABE scheme, in which an attribute merging algorithm is designed to avoid the exponential growth in computational latency and overhead caused by continuous verification of CE-ZTA. Theoretical analysis and simulation results show that the proposed scheme complies with ZTA principles and is superior to existing CP-ABE schemes in terms of latency, overhead, and robustness.

title: IEEE INTELLIGENT TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS SOCIETY
authors: N/A
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems

title: ICSP 2026 TOC
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)

title: Joint Security–Energy–Latency Optimization for Code Offloading in LEO Satellite Networks with Mixed Security Requirements
authors: Jeongsoo Kim, Suhyeon Jeon, Jeongho Kwak, Song Chong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Communications
abstract: Satellite edge computing has emerged as a promising paradigm to support global-scale low-latency services by enabling remote code execution in orbit. However, unlike terrestrial networks, satellite communication is fundamentally vulnerable to eavesdropping due to its wide broadcast range, high accessibility, and long-distance propagation, which is further exacerbated by the high altitudes allowing a vast set of potential eavesdropper positions. Despite these unique risks, most prior works have either ignored security or treated it as a uniform concern across services. In this paper, we take an initial step toward secure satellite edge computing by explicitly modeling and optimizing for security heterogeneity, i.e., diverse security demands across different services and dynamic jamming strategies. We formulate a novel optimization problem that jointly minimizes average information leakage for security-sensitive services and overall energy consumption while meeting latency constraints. To solve it, we propose a stochastic optimization-based algorithm, namely SOS, which dynamically controls code offloading, resource allocation, and jamming strategy with respect to service-specific security requirements. Extensive simulations show that SOS outperforms existing schemes by achieving up to a 92% reduction in average information leakage with only an 18% increase in energy consumption at the same latency compared to an existing algorithm. Moreover, SOS maintains high jamming efficiency and leakage robustness under both colluding and non-colluding attacks. Our findings highlight a fundamental yet overlooked security-energy-latency trade-off and position SOS as a pioneering step toward security-aware satellite edge computing.

title: Design of Random Forest-Based Low-Power VLSI Architecture to Detect Congestive Heart Failure for Wearable Devices
authors: Abhyuday Bhardwaj, Meenali Janveja, Srinivasan Krishnaswamy, Jan Pidanic, Gaurav Trivedi
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: Congestive heart failure (CHF) is an acute syndrome that results from ventricular dysfunction and progresses in four stages. Its timely detection can reverse heart damage and save lives. This work proposes a low-power, computationally efficient VLSI architecture to detect CHF using a single-lead ECG signal for the first time. This architecture employs a novel wavelet function-based electrocardiogram (ECG) feature extraction method and a random forest (RF) classifier, which can classify regular beats from CHF beats using a subject-oriented approach. Using ECG signals from publicly available datasets, BIDMC-CHF and MIT-BIH NSRDB, the proposed architecture achieves 90.5% accuracy, having a power consumption of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$0.1~\mu W$</tex-math> </inline-formula> when implemented using TSMC 40-nm bulk CMOS technology as an ASIC. The low power consumption of the proposed architecture enables it to be utilized efficiently for real-time ECG analysis in wearable devices.

title: Federated Learning via Variational Bayesian Inference: Personalization, Sparsity and Clustering_supp1-3699626.pdf
authors: Yinchuan Li
publish: N/A
abstract: Federated learning (FL) is a promising framework that models distributed machine learning while protecting the privacy of clients. However, FL suffers performance degradation from heterogeneous and limited data. To alleviate the degradation, we present a novel personalized Bayesian FL approach named pFedBayes. By using the trained global distribution from the server as the prior distribution of each client, each client adjusts its own distribution by minimizing the sum of the reconstruction error over its personalized data and the KL divergence with the downloaded global distribution. Then, we propose a sparse personalized Bayesian FL approach named sFedBayes to enhance the inference efficiency. To overcome the extreme heterogeneity in non-i.i.d. data, we propose a clustered Bayesian FL model named cFedbayes by learning different prior distributions for different clients. Theoretical analysis gives the generalization error bound of three approaches and shows that the generalization error rates of the proposed approaches achieve minimax optimality up to a logarithmic factor. Moreover, cFedBayes achieves a cluster-level generalization error bound, rather than a single uniform bound in pFedBayes. Numerous experiments demonstrate that the proposed approaches have better performance than other advanced personalized methods on private models in the presence of heterogeneous and limited data.

title: Enhancing the long-term reliability of quartz resonant force sensors via a Ti interlayer: a combined DFT and experimental study
authors: Chang-chun Zhao, Qiu-shi Liu, Cheng-yi Wu, Gang-yin Ding, Shih-yung Pao, Shih-chun Chen, Chin-yin Chen, Min-Chiang Chao
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Quartz resonant force sensors, known for their high resolution and fast response, impose stringent requirements on the stability of their packaging structure. This study focuses on the stability of the Fe-epoxy-SiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub> bonding interface in quartz sensors, systematically investigating the role of Ti as a transition layer at the Fe-epoxy interface through a combination of DFT simulations and experimental characterization. Simulation results indicate that the Fe-epoxy interface exhibits poor bonding stability, whereas Ti forms stable chemical bonds with both Fe and epoxy. Ti layers with thicknesses of 100 nm and 500 nm were respectively deposited onto the Fe frame surface using magnetron sputtering technology. FIB-SEM and step profilometry confirmed the successful introduction of the Ti transition layer at the Fe-epoxy interface. In 10,000 loading-unloading durability cycle tests, the sample with a 500 nm Ti layer demonstrated significantly better performance in terms of indication error, repeatability, and hysteresis error compared to both the 100 nm Ti sample and the sample without Ti. Specifically, hysteresis performance was improved by over 90%, while the stability of repeatability and indication error was enhanced by approximately 30% and over 50%, respectively. The formation of this stable interface enables the sensor to effectively transmit loads during force application, fundamentally alleviating the relative slippage issues caused by the unstable Fe-epoxy interface.

title: Beyond Correlation: Causal Intervention for Multi-Label Medical Image Diagnosis_supp1-3698052.pdf
authors: Yalin Zheng
publish: N/A
abstract: This paper addresses the challenge of multi-disease diagnosis by integrating causal reasoning into the diagnostic framework. In clinical practice, multiple conditions often co-occur, making multi-disease diagnosis more relevant than isolated single-disease cases. However, most deep learning methods focus on single-disease detection and fail to capture the complexity of diagnosing concurrent conditions. Even in multi-label settings, existing approaches mainly rely on correlation-based inference, capturing statistical associations rather than true causal relationships. This can lead to spurious feature-disease associations, where features linked to one disease are mistakenly attributed to another due to frequent co-occurrence, ultimately undermines diagnostic accuracy and interpretability. To address this challenge, we propose a novel framework that incorporates causal intervention into multi-label medical image diagnosis, enabling the model to identify true causal signals rather than misleading correlations arising from co-occurring diseases. Specifically, we model latent disease-related confounders and apply backdoor adjustment to disentangle genuine causal effects from spurious associations. This is achieved by implicitly learning shared feature representations that serve as confounding variables, which are then used to refine image-derived features during prediction. The resulting causal adjustment allows the model to focus on disease-specific cues, improving accuracy and interpretability. Extensive experiments on four diverse medical imaging datasets: ODIR (color fundus photography), LID-FFA (fundus fluorescein angiography), Endo (colonoscopy), and Chestpert (X-ray) demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms existing approaches. Furthermore, our model also effectively separates the diagnosis of co-occurring diseases, high-lighting the potential of causal reasoning to enhance the reliability and clinical applicability of AI-assisted diagnosis. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/davelailai/BankCausal.git.

title: Twining Stems Inspired Soft Actuators with High Aspect Ratio: Design, Characterization, and Application_supp4-3697813.avi
authors: Jingjing Feng
publish: N/A
abstract: Inspired by the helical growth strategy of natural twining stems, this study introduces a pneumatically driven Soft Twining-stem-inspired Actuator (STA) with high aspect ratio (> 20:1) and thin-walled structures. Fabricated via an integrated dip-molding and fiber-reinforced process, the STA achieves 3D spatial curling deformation with only a single pneumatic chamber, eliminating the need for multi-chamber coordination. The establishment of mechanical model identified the key parameters governing its deformation, which was then validated through performance testing and analysis. This revealed that fiber thread density regulation enable up to 67.1 mm radial contraction (33.6% length) and 1021° rotation at 80 kPa. Practical applications demonstrate STA’s versatility across multiple domains. The actuators function effectively as artificial muscles to drive a robotic elbow, and demonstrate a snap-through mechanism which facilitates rapid bidirectional motion switching. Beyond actuation applications, soft universal grippers composed of 6 STAs achieve non-destructive grasping of both common objects and irregularly shaped targets, highlighting the system’s multi-functional versatility. Furthermore, a pipe-crawling robot was constructed utilizing STAs, showcasing the ability to generate effective anisotropic friction for locomotion within confined pipelines. Overall, this study aims to establish STAs as a versatile actuator design, offering insights for simplified and high - performance soft robotics.

title: Multi-Objective Joint Optimization of Deep Network: For Online Learning of Streaming Data With Concept Drift
authors: Husheng Guo, Jingnan Su, Cong Shen, Wenjian Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Knowledge and Data Engineering
abstract: Concept drift poses a challenge in the field of data stream mining. Most existing online deep learning methods rely on a single objective, failing to sufficiently capture the latent feature information embedded in the data, which hinders rapid adaptation to distribution changes. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Multi-objective Joint Optimization of Deep Network (MJOD) model. Specifically, the feature connection network (FCN) connects features from different layers via dense feature units, constructing an integrated classification objective to fully utilize both shallow and deep information for precise prediction. Subsequently, the feature refinement network (FRN) compares historical representative information with current data to build a compressed reconstruction objective, thereby capturing changes in data distribution. Furthermore, the feature enhancement network (FEN) extracts spatial structure information from the data and constructs an information balance objective to retain the information most relevant to the task. By jointly optimizing the integrated classification, compressed reconstruction, and information balance objectives, the model maximizes the utilization of feature information within the streaming data, enabling rapid learning of new distributions. Experimental results demonstrate that MJOD is superior to other baseline methods across various drift scenarios.

title: Retraction Notice: Dynamic Graph Neural Network Learning for Temporal Omics Data Prediction
authors: Xiaoli Jing, Yanhong Zhou, Min Shi
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: High-throughput studies of biological systems are rapidly generating a wealth of ‘omics’-scale data. Many of these studies are temporal collecting proteomics and genomics data capturing dynamic observations. While temporal omics data are essential to unravel the mechanisms of various diseases, they often include missing (or incomplete) values due to technical and experimental reasons. Data prediction methods, i.e., imputation and forecasting, have been widely used to mitigate these issues. However, existing imputation and forecasting techniques typically address static omics data representing a single time point and perform forecasting on data with complete values. In this paper, we propose a graph-based method for temporal omics data imputation and forecasting that handle omics data containing missing values at multiple time points. The method takes advantage of topological relationships (e.g., protein-protein and gene-gene interactions) among omics data samples and incorporates a graph convolutional network to first infer the missing values at different time points. Then, we combine these inferred values with the original omics data to perform temporal imputation and forecasting using a long short-term memory network. Evaluating the proposed method on two real-world datasets demonstrated a distinct advantage over existing data imputation and forecasting methods. On the omics dataset, the average mean square error of our method improved 11.3% for imputation and 6.4% for forecasting compared to the baseline methods.

title: SCOUT: LLM-driven Vulnerability Detection via Interactive Repository Exploration
authors: Heping Gao, Di Zhang, Liyan Chen
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: Detecting software vulnerabilities requires understanding not only the target code but also its broader repository context. Existing LLM-based approaches either operate on isolated functions or rely on heavyweight program analysis such as Code Property Graphs, leaving them caught between insufficient context and information overload.We propose SCOUT (Structure-guided CodeObservation and Understanding), an interactive agent-based framework that lets LLMs actively explore repository structures for vulnerability detection. SCOUT organizes the repository in two layers: a file-level structure graph for navigation and a function-level index for fine-grained retrieval within each file. Given a target function, SCOUT initializes a local subgraph centered on its containing file and allows the LLM to iteratively request additional context through a progressive subgraph expansion mechanism.We evaluate SCOUT on PrimeVul over C/C++ projects. On pair-wise discrimination, the metric PrimeVul’s authors introduced to test whether a model genuinely distinguishes vulnerable code from its patch, SCOUT reports the highest P-C (108/435) and VP-S (69) to date, alongside the highest accuracy (57.93%) and the lowest false positive rate (41.84%) among existing baselines, all without any training. To probe generality, we further report a same-backbone comparison against function-only and static-context prompting, and a backbone-sensitivity study across four LLMs. Our results suggest that effective vulnerability detection does not require complex program analysis representations, but rather an efficient navigation mechanism that lets LLMs acquire the right information at the right time.We acknowledge that the absolute level on this rigorously curated benchmark remains modest, and position SCOUT as a triage component rather than an unattended detector.

title: Clinically Meaningful Phonocardiogram Denoising with Feature Aware Fine-Tuning for CHD Detection
authors: Joaquim Bauxell, Declan Duggan, Sergi Gómez-Quintana, Victoriya Shelevytska, Andreea Factor, Andriy Temko, Emanuel Popovici
publish: IEEE Sensors Journal
abstract: Phonocardiograms (PCGs) in conjunction with AI are increasingly used for detecting heart and lung pathologies. However, they are often corrupted by artifacts such as patient motion, background conversations, and equipment alarms, which can obscure cardiac murmurs critical for detecting congenital heart disease (CHD). In this work, we examine how denoising affects subsequent automatic CHD classification, and propose some considerations aimed to preserve a group of features commonly used by clinical classifiers. Our study couples a UNet-based spectrogram denoiser, pretrained on phonocardiogram recordings, with a clinically validated XGBoost screening model operating on interpretable, structured features derived from spectrograms. We show that applying a pretrained denoiser without proper adaptation substantially alters the distributions of clinically relevant features and degrades detection performance, despite achieving strong waveform reconstruction quality. Various fine-tuning strategies are examined to guide denoising toward preserving relevant features, starting with variations of the reconstruction loss, including Huber, L1, log-domain, and frequency-weighted objectives, preserving up to ≈92% of the diagnostic performance at 0 dB SNR compared to the observed on unaltered signals. Finally, we introduce a method for jointly training a convolutional denoiser and detector without phase annotations, with matching diagnostic performance in exchange for reduced interpretability. These results demonstrate that favorable reconstruction metrics alone (e.g., SI-SDR up to 14.77 dB at 0 dB SNR) are insufficient indicators of clinical fidelity, and that denoising strategies for PCG analysis should be explicitly aligned with downstream diagnostic objectives to ensure robust CHD screening.

title: World Models for Cognitive Agents: Transforming Edge Intelligence in Future Networks
authors: Changyuan Zhao, Ruichen Zhang, Jiacheng Wang, Gaosheng Zhao, Dusit Niyato, Geng Sun, Shiwen Mao, Dong In Kim
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: World models are emerging as a transformative paradigm in artificial intelligence, enabling agents to construct internal representations of their environments for predictive reasoning, planning, and decision-making. By learning latent dynamics, world models provide a sample-efficient framework that is especially valuable in data-constrained or safety-critical scenarios. In this article, we present a comprehensive overview of world models, highlighting their architecture, training paradigms, and applications across prediction, generation, planning, and causal reasoning. We compare and distinguish world models from related concepts such as digital twins, the metaverse, and foundation models, clarifying their unique role as embedded cognitive engines for autonomous agents. We further propose Wireless Dreamer, a novel world model-based reinforcement learning framework tailored for wireless edge intelligence optimization, particularly in low-altitude wireless networks (LAWNs). Through a weather-aware UAV trajectory planning case study, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework in improving learning efficiency and decision quality.
