title: A GRO-TDC-Based Digital PLL Featuring Single-Tone Spur Cancellation
authors: Yan Chen, Hanli Liu, Jifeng Zhang, Yu Cui, Luyao Gao, Xugang Ke, Xiang Gao
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: Power supply noise, reference clock jitter, and coupling interference introduce unpredictable spurs in the phase-locked loop (PLL) output spectrum, significantly degrading system performance. This letter presents a digital PLL (DPLL) architecture utilizing a gated ring oscillator time-to-digital converter (GRO-TDC) with a spur cancellation technique. Without interrupting normal PLL operation, the proposed technique effectively suppresses spurs through a two-stage method comprising background precompensation and foreground closed-loop fine-tuning. Measurement results from a 55-nm CMOS prototype show a 25–26dB spur suppression across an offset frequency range of 312.5kHz to 1.25MHz.

title: On Using Microstrip Transmission Lines to Implement Transformed Electromagnetic Media
authors: Arun Govindankutty, Anna Rudie, Benjamin D. Braaten, Monica Allen, Jeffery Allen
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: This letter presents an experimental demonstration of reactively loaded microstrip structures that emulate the compression and expansion of electromagnetic waves within the transformation electromagnetics (TEs) framework. The TE interpretation is used as an analytical model to compute the permittivity and permeability matrices for expansion and compression medium in the longitudinal direction of wave propagation at 1.0 GHz. Transmission-line theory is then employed to design and synthesize periodic unit cells that modify the effective per-unit-length inductance and capacitance, thereby controlling the propagation constant and accumulated phase, and hence emulating the permittivity and permeability of the transformed space. The proposed design achieves an impedance transformation from 377 to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$50~\Omega $</tex-math> </inline-formula> through the specific printed circuit board unit cells representing air, wave-compression, and wave-expansion media. Theoretical predictions for the individual media and composite media are validated using advanced design systems (ADS) simulations. Measurements of the fabricated prototypes show very good agreement with the designed response, confirming the feasibility of the proposed transformation prototyping approach.

title: Analysis of Severe Positive Threshold Voltage Shift in IZO Thin-Film Transistors Under High-Temperature and High-Bias Stress
authors: Peibin Zhang, Ce Ning, Zhonghao Huang, Lizhong Wang, Rui Huang, Lei Zhou, Shuai Wu, Aoxue Yang, Min Li, Miao Xu, Lei Wang, Guangcai Yuan, Zichao Liu, Junbiao Peng, Hongwei Du
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: Even though the self-heating (SH) effect and the hot carrier (HC) effect have been proposed to account for the severe positive threshold voltage (<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {th}}\text {)}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> shift of indium-zinc oxide (IZO) thin-film transistors (TFTs) under high-temperature and high-bias stress, the location and the defect type are unknown. Assisted by TCAD simulation, the density of acceptor-like tail states <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${N}_{\text {TA}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and negative interface charge density <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${Q}_{\text {F}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> at the backchannel interface are determined to be the major defects responsible for the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {th}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> shift. The HC and the SH effects degrade the passivation layer’s compactness, making the penetration of water vapor and oxygen into the backchannel easy. <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${N}_{\text {TA}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> and <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${Q}_{\text {F}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> impact the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {th}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> by capturing electrons and generating a negative electric field, respectively, which need higher <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${V}_{\text {GS}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> to counteract.

title: Output Performance of a Reciprocating Electrothermal Mechanism With Cooperative U- and V-Shaped Actuators
authors: Haikang Xu, Mo Yang, Xinjie Wang, Yun Cao
publish: Journal of Microelectromechanical Systems
abstract: To meet the requirements of reciprocating actuation for interrupters in fuze safety systems, a reciprocating electrothermal driving mechanism based on the cooperative operation of U-shaped and V-shaped electrothermal actuators is proposed. The designed mechanism achieves large output displacement under low power input, while satisfying the demands of reversibility, recoverability, and miniaturization required in safety and arming devices. Amultiphysics-coupled finite element model is established to systematically investigate the steady-state output performance and dynamic response of the actuator. In addition, an experimental platform was constructed to validate the actuation characteristics of the proposed mechanism. Within a single working cycle, the electrothermal actuation mechanism can achieve a maximum displacement of <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$174.6~\upmu $</tex-math> </inline-formula>m at 2V, corresponding to an average stepping speed of approximately 0.46mm/s, while the actuator-level response times remain below 150ms in repeated experiments. Both simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed reciprocating electrothermal actuator exhibits a compact structure, high actuation efficiency, and stable dynamic response, providing a feasible reference for the intelligent and miniaturized design of next-generation fuze safety mechanisms. [2026-0032]

title: Toward Real-time High-fidelity Immersive Robotic Telepresence via Online 3D Gaussian Splatting
authors: Jingxin Du, Mo Xu, Yaxin Hu, Xuebin Sun, Bilge Mutlu, Kevin Ponto
publish: IEEE Robotics and Automation Letters
abstract: Immersive robotic telepresence offers new possibilities for users to feel like they are present in remote locations. This provides benefits for many application areas, such as remote inspection, virtual tourism, and hazardous environment monitoring. However, due to limitations in their underlying technologies, architecture design, and hardware constraints, existing robotic telepresence systems often force a trade-off between visual quality, system performance, and display immersiveness. Furthermore, much of the existing research exhibits a narrow focus, often centering on improving and evaluating a single technical aspect, such as reconstruction fidelity or rendering speed, while failing to capture the holistic user experience. We present a novel online 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)-based virtual reality (VR) robotic telepresence system that more effectively balances these competing objectives through a careful co-design spanning the system's algorithms, architecture, and pipelines across the reconstruction, rendering, and streaming processes. We also introduce a comprehensive evaluation framework that goes beyond reconstruction fidelity and rendering speed to evaluate key interaction-critical factors such as convergence speed and system latency. Empirical results demonstrate that our system outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in terms of both fidelity and performance, while also providing an immersive experience.

title: A Wideband Ultra-Large-Angle Bandpass Frequency Selective Surface With TE-TM Polarization-Consistent Response
authors: Jiaxin Wang, Xin Xiu, Weiwei Men, Bao Wang, Mengzhu Yan, Menglin Xu, Ye Han, Wenquan Che
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: This work proposes a wideband, ultra-large-angle bandpass frequency selective surface (FSS) for aircraft conformal radome applications. Considering the divergent TE- and TM-polarized wave impedance, a polarization-decoupling design methodology is employed, enabling orthogonal low cross-polarization structures to achieve matching for TE and TM polarizations independently. Based on this strategy, distinct angle-stable electromagnetic (EM) resonators are tailored for TE and TM polarizations, while maintaining a moderate unit period for practical design. Using frequency-angle two-domain equivalent circuit models (ECMs), this work indicates that these resonators exhibit angle-insensitive equivalent circuit parameters (ECPs). Furthermore, this characteristic enables efficient analysis and design of wideband angle-stable TE- and TM-polarized FSSs with similar magnitude responses, achieving polarization consistency in terms of transmission magnitude. For polarization consistency in terms of transmission phase, this work extends the existing effective medium theory (EMT) by deriving the phase consistency condition and successfully applies it to align transmission phase at large incident angles. Simulation results show that across 50°–80° incidence, the proposed FSS achieves a dual-polarization 3-dB passband of 7–13 GHz (60%), with a TE-TM transmission phase difference (TPD) of less than 25°. A prototype was fabricated and measured. The measured results agree well with simulations, validating the proposed design strategy.

title: Securing the Distributed LLM Pipeline: From Risk Assessment to an Efficient Spatial-Temporal Defense Mechanism
authors: Bizhu Wang, Ziyun Wei, Honghao Liu, Weiyu Jiang, Xiaodong Xu, Ping Zhang, Yinqiu Liu, Dusit Niyato
publish: IEEE Communications Magazine
abstract: Distributed parallel frameworks are widely adopted in modern large language model (LLM) systems to facilitate computation, but expose critical data such as exchanged intermediate activations and gradients to on-path attackers. Although cryptographic encryption and verification can prevent such attacks, but securing all in-transit data inevitably introduces substantial latency and degrades system efficiency. To address this challenge, this article provides a tutorial on quantifying on-path tampering and privacy leakage vulnerabilities in distributed LLM computation. Using Qwen2.5-7B and GPT-2 as examples, this article reveals that attack impact decreases with model layer depth, motivating the proposed risk-adaptive security controls that adjust protection intensity based on the spatial depth of each model layer. Furthermore, this article proposes leveraging asynchronous key generation and pipelined security processing to hide security operation-induced latency within the computation and communication pipeline. In this way, the above strategies enable a spatial-temporal co-optimization defense that balances security and efficiency, offering a promising solution for secure and high-performance distributed LLM deployment.

title: Reliable Lane-Level Localization for Autonomous Vehicles With Enhanced HMM-Based Map Matching and Lane-Line Assistance
authors: Shihao Zhang, Jing Yang, Ziyu Song, Zhaochen Xia, Neeraj Kumar, Lip Yee Por, Thippa Reddy Gadekallu, Haitao Ding
publish: IEEE Transactions on Vehicular Technology
abstract: Lane-level high-precision localization is essential for autonomous driving, yet it remains highly challenging under Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS) signal degradation and complex urban road conditions. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a unified low-cost localization framework that relies on a GNSS receiver, a MEMS-grade IMU, and a forward-facing camera. The proposed system integrates an Extended Kalman Particle Filter (EKPF), an enhanced Hidden Markov Model (HMM) based map-matching algorithm, and a vision-based lane-line-assisted correction module. The enhanced HMM incorporates a hierarchical road-to-lane search, an adaptive intersection-aware state transition model, and a multi-criteria lane-change detection mechanism to ensure reliable performance in dense urban environments. Building upon stable lane identification, the lane-line-assisted module utilizes camera-detected geometric features to correct lateral drift and improve robustness under GNSS degradation. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate that, while meeting real-time requirements, the proposed system achieves over 92% lane-level matching accuracy and maintains above 91% accuracy under GNSS degradation conditions with up to 4.0 m GNSS noise. The mean positioning error reaches 0.29 m under urban canyon conditions, confirming the framework's robustness, accuracy, and practicality for low-cost lane-level autonomous vehicle localization.

title: Metro Wayside Energy Storage Systems Sizing Optimization Considering the Spatiotemporal Coupling Characteristics of ‘Vehicle-Grid-Storage’
authors: Xinjie Zheng, Jian Wu, Chi Zhang, Zhenlin Zhu, Lei Wu, Chenguang Fan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
abstract: The sizing optimization for wayside energy storage systems (ESS) is considered an effective way to reduce energy consumption in the metro traction power supply system and improve the utilization rate of regenerative braking energy (RBE). However, existing research primarily focuses on the bilevel optimization approach of capacity sizing and energy management, or convex programming simplified by dividing the system into separate power supply zones. These algorithms neglect the complex coupling relationships between subsystems, thereby compromising model fidelity and overall optimality. Therefore, this paper formulates an optimization of ESS sizing to minimize the system’s lifecycle cost, which considers the spatiotemporal coupling characteristics between the train system, traction power supply system, and ESS, transforming the energy management problem into an optimal power flow calculation problem. Then, by introducing convex relaxation and linearization, we propose a second-order cone programming (SOCP) to reformulate the original non-convex problem into a convex problem that can be solved quickly. Case studies comparing multiple methods validate the effectiveness of the proposed method. The results demonstrate that the proposed method reduces the lifecycle cost by 27.28% compared to the unoptimized method, and by 4.47% relative to the traditional rule-based bi-level optimization method. Moreover, the computation time is reduced from ”hours” to ”seconds”, which significantly improves computational efficiency.

title: Bio-inspired Optofluidic Molecular Communication with Photothermally Actuated Microrobot Swarms_supp3-3698265.mp4
authors: Tingting Yu
publish: N/A
abstract: Molecular communication (MC) offers a bio-inspired paradigm for information transfer in environments inaccessible to conventional electromagnetic waves. However, translating MC concepts to the microscale has been hampered by a lack of integrated, biocompatible testbeds. Inspired by biological spectral-dependent photothermal transduction of specific light wavelengths into thermal energy, we present the first fully integrated microscopic MC platform utilizing photothermally responsive microrobot swarms. Our platform employs core-shell microrobots that exhibit a strong photothermal response, enabling precise and non-invasive navigation within microfluidic channels via near-infrared (NIR) light. This optofluidic architecture facilitates a symbiotic dual-bit encoding scheme, which concurrently modulates information onto both microrobot arrival and the optical control states. We demonstrate a complete communication workflow, from microrobot emission and laser-guided modulation to real-time optical detection and signal demodulation. The system achieves a data rate of 0.63 bits · min−1 with a low bit error rate of 4%, validated by a multi-sampling detection algorithm and the transmission of the ASCII string “HELLO WORLD”. This work provides a robust testbed for validating MC theories in biologically relevant microenvironments and serves as a step toward applications in the Internet of Bio-Nano Things.

title: Lyapunov-guided Latency Optimization in Semantic Communication Empowered Edge Networks
authors: Yang Li, Chen Chen, Li Feng, Jiandian Zeng, Tian Wang, Yuan Wu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Mobile Computing
abstract: The rapid proliferation of data-intensive applications has exacerbated the limitations of traditional communication systems, which treat data as meaningless symbols leading to inefficient transmission. Semantic communication emerges as a promising paradigm that transmits the meaning of information rather than raw bits, significantly reducing bandwidth consumption while maintaining task performance. However, existing Semantic Communication empowered Edge Networks (SCEN) overlook two critical challenges, namely, the transmission efficiency subject to semantic reconstruction quality constraints and the impact of user mobility on computation offloading policies. To address these gaps, this paper proposes a comprehensive optimization framework that jointly optimizes semantic feature selection and bandwidth allocation to minimize transmission latency while ensuring semantic quality. For static scenarios, a hybrid approach combining Karush–Kuhn–Tucker conditions and Harris Hawks optimization is developed. For dynamic environments with mobile users, a Lyapunov-guided stability aware formulation is further introduced, a novel distributed and cooperative multi-agent proximal policy optimization algorithm that incorporates a contribution-based global reward and fairness-driven penalty mechanism. Extensive experiments on real-world mobility data and the CIFAR-10 dataset demonstrate that the proposed methods achieve superior performances in terms of latency reduction, queue stability, and semantic quality compared to state-of-the-art baselines, validating the efficacy and scalability in both static and dynamic SCEN scenarios.

title: Zonotopic Fault Detection for Space Tethered Satellite Fuzzy System Based on Polynomial Chaos Expansion
authors: Lanshuang Zhang, Zhenhua Wang, Xiaolei Li, Ali Zolghadri, Yi Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Fuzzy Systems
abstract: This paper develops an observer-based fault detection scheme for Space Tethered Satellite (STS) systems, integrating Polynomial Chaos Expansion (PCE) with a zono topic approach. To manage system complexity, the STS is first modeled using a Takagi-Sugeno (T-S) fuzzy framework with local Lipschitz function. A robust observer is then designed using H∞ optimization techniques, employing a generalized method to handle Lipschitz nonlinearity with fewer restrictions than traditional approaches. By combining PCE with zono topes, the scheme simultaneously accounts for time-invariant probabilistic uncertainties and set-based bounded uncertainties. This hybrid quantification reduces the inherent conservatism of purely bound-based methods, yielding more accurate estimation results. Finally, fault detection is achieved by monitoring the residual-containing zonotopic sets. Numerical simulations on an STS system demonstrate the effectiveness and accuracy of the proposed methodology.

title: Non-Invasive Blood Pressure Estimation Using PPG and a CNN-BiLSTM-Attention Deep Learning Architecture_supp1-3699556.docx
authors: Bahram Tarvirdizadeh
publish: N/A

title: Digital Transformation of University Political Education Courses Using Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning
authors: Wentai Zhang
publish: 2025 International Conference on Low-Altitude Airspace and Artificial Intelligence (LAAI)
abstract: The integration of artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning (ML) into university political education addresses key challenges, including low student engagement, limited adaptability to diverse learning needs, and reliance on traditional teaching methods. This study presents a predictive analytics framework designed to optimize political education by leveraging clustering algorithms, decision trees, and neural networks to process and analyze educational data. By utilizing data from Learning Management Systems (LMS), discussion forums, and digital learning tools, the framework enables personalized interventions and adaptive learning experiences. Experimental results reveal that AI-driven approaches significantly enhance learning outcomes, with forum participation rates increasing by 35% and quiz completion rates improving by 27% compared to traditional methods. Additionally, early warning systems built on predictive analytics identified at-risk students with high precision, facilitating timely interventions and achieving an 18% improvement in retention rates. These findings demonstrate the transformative potential of predictive analytics in fostering engagement, improving retention, and enabling dynamic adjustments to course content. By bridging computational technologies and pedagogy, this study establishes a robust and scalable framework for the digital transformation of political education, offering new possibilities to create inclusive and effective learning environments.

title: Simulation Study on Precise Identification of Composite Spectral Signals Based on Neural Network Model
authors: Shichao Kuai, Ningning Li, Wei Zheng
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: The tunable diode laser absorption spectroscopy (TDLAS) technology is the core method for online detection of multi-component gases. However, the overlapping interference of multi-gas composite spectral signals, baseline drift and noise pollution are the key problems restricting the detection accuracy. To meet the spectral separation requirements of a mixed gas of carbon monoxide (CO), oxygen (O<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>), methane (CH<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</inf>), and hydrogen sulfide (H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</inf>S), this paper constructs a precise identification model for composite spectral signals based on a feedforward neural network. By generating a dataset of four-component mixed spectral data with noise through simulation, pre-processing operations such as data normalization and dataset division are completed; a double-hidden-layer neural network structure is designed, and the scaled conjugate gradient algorithm is used to complete model training; the model is quantitatively evaluated using the mean square error (MSE) and the coefficient of determination (R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>), and the visualization of spectral separation results is achieved. The simulation results show that this model can achieve high-precision decoupling and separation of composite spectra.

title: Fusion of Computer Vision for Industrial Design Image Recognition and Intelligent Assisted Design System Research
authors: Airexiati Abudureheman, Yuhan Ning, Yihang Luo, Dixin Pan, Tianyi Wang, Yuwen Gao, Jianyi Zhang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Industrial design still relies heavily on manual visual inspection, fragmented reference libraries, and repeated trial-and-error modification. To address these limitations, this paper develops a computer-vision-driven intelligent assistance system for industrial design image recognition and controllable design generation. The framework unifies multimodal perception, semantic parsing, cross-modal retrieval, and structured generation in a single pipeline. A hybrid ConvNeXt-Swin encoder extracts complementary local contour, texture, and part-level cues from product images, sketches, and CAD renders; a multi-task head jointly predicts category labels, style-material attributes, and semantic masks of reusable elements. In parallel, a CLIP-aligned branch projects visual observations and textual prompts into a shared retrieval space and links them to a design-element database. The generation module then reuses segmentation masks and attribute tokens as spatial controls for variant synthesis rather than unconstrained text-to-image creation. Experiments on a curated industrial design benchmark show 94.3% top-1 recognition accuracy, 84.9% mIoU for part parsing, a 65% reduction in average concept iteration time, and clear gains in retrieval precision and variant consistency over ResNet50, ConvNeXt-T, and Swin-T baselines. The results indicate that intelligent industrial design assistance should be formulated as a tightly coupled computation problem involving representation learning, segmentation, retrieval, and human-in-the-loop generation.

title: Reinforcement Learning-Based Finite-Time Optimal Control for Nonlinear Systems with State Constraints and Input Time Delays
authors: Ziwen Xu, Yichen Li
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: This paper investigates the finite-time optimal control problem for a class of nonlinear systems with symmetric time-invariant constraints and input time delays. Firstly, based on the definition of finite time and reinforcement learning algorithm, an Sub-actor-critic network framework is constructed by using the optimal backstepping method. Under this framework, the tracking error and system state variables are always confined within the prescribed constraint boundaries. Secondly, an intermediate variable is introduced, and the input time delay is approximated by means of Padé approximation. Furthermore, a policy iteration algorithm is employed to synchronously update the control inputs and value functions of each subsystem, so as to solve the corresponding HJB equations. Finally, simulation results further verify the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: DGCRAB: Dynamic-Gated CRAB for Magnetic Tile Defect Classification
authors: Chengzhi Yan
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Magnetic tile defects exhibit significant scale variation and weak signal characteristics, which challenge conventional attention mechanisms. Existing models such as CRAB rely on fixed receptive fields and global average pooling, leading to limited adaptability and loss of high-frequency information. We propose DGCRAB, a dynamic-gated extension of CRAB that integrates two modules: a Dynamic-Gated Multi-scale Reception Module (DMRM) for input-adaptive receptive-field selection, and an Enhanced Global Attention Module (EGAM) that combines global average pooling and global max pooling to capture complementary channel statistics. DMRM is interpretable as a lightweight mixture-of-experts over dilation scales, while EGAM preserves high-frequency cues crucial for weak defects. With weighted sampling, Focal Loss, and class-aware augmentation, DGCRAB achieves 99.80% accuracy on the AMT dataset and 80.30% on GC10-DET, demonstrating strong generalization.

title: ADC-Net: A Deep Adversarial Decoupling and Contrastive Learning Framework for Cross-Parameter Radar Specific Emitter Identification
authors: Wanze Wang, Jifei Pan
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: In the realm of modern cognitive electronic warfare and complex electromagnetic spectrum sensing, Specific Emitter Identification (SEI) has emerged as an indispensable physical-layer technology for extracting unintentional modulation features. These distinctive Radio Frequency Fingerprints (RFFs) are inherent in radar signals due to inevitable hardware imperfections. However, as advanced radar systems increasingly adopt parameter-agile operational modes, traditional SEI paradigms encounter significant theoretical challenges. The dynamic adjustment of macroscopic parameters results in pronounced intra-class waveform divergence. To address the shortcomings of conventional generative models in cross-parameter scenarios, this study introduces a novel discriminative architecture: the Adversarial Decoupling Contrastive Network (ADC-Net). By moving away from the flawed generative reconstruction paradigm, ADC-Net establishes a unified framework that combines supervised contrastive learning with adversarial gradient reversal. Empirical experiments conducted on measured multi-parameter radar datasets with same-type hardware emitters demonstrate that ADC-Net achieves an impressive 99.75% identity recognition accuracy and a 97.66% parameter classification accuracy. The framework effectively distills parameter-invariant micro-hardware fingerprints, achieving highly orthogonal mathematical decoupling between identity and parameter features, while addressing the inherent limitations of generative disentanglement paradigms.

title: A Certificateless Group Signature with Fault-Tolerant Batch Verification for VANETs
authors: Xinlong Wang, Siyuan Chen, Weiwei Li
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: As Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) develop rapidly, Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks (VANETs) play a key role in enhancing road safety and traffic management efficiency. To address the challenges of identity anonymity and communication security in the large-scale real-time data exchange within VANETs, this paper proposes a VANET anonymous authentication system based on a fault-tolerant group signature scheme. This system not only resolves the issues of complex certificate management and insufficient verification efficiency inherent in traditional group signature schemes but also enhances system robustness by introducing a batch verification fault-tolerant mechanism. Additionally, it reduces communication and computational overhead by constructing a hierarchical matrix positioning algorithm for rapid detection of invalid signatures. Theoretical analysis and experimental validation demonstrate that the proposed approach satisfies the requirements for both anonymous authentication security and efficiency in vehicular networks.

title: Simulation and Prediction of Urban Land Based on CA-Markov During the Process of Urban Renewal in Fuxin
authors: Chunhua Wu, Peiquan Huang, Yi Chen, Xianyin Qiu
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Perception and Autonomous Control (IPAC)
abstract: Over the past decades, Fuxin has experienced significant changes in urban land use driven by economic transformation. By examining urban land dynamics during past renewal processes, a more accurate future development direction for Fuxin can be identified. Based on the Kappa indices derived from urban land data for 1997, 2001, 2006, and 2010, the changes in Fuxin's urban land show a gradually increasing trend. Furthermore, using the four transition rules of landscape types for the periods 1997-2001, 2001-2006, 2006-2010, and 2001-2010, the CA-Markov model was applied to simulate and predict changes in urban land use. The CA-Markov model is a classic coupled model widely used for simulating and predicting land use and land cover change (LULC). It integrates the strengths of Markov chains in quantitative prediction with the ability of cellular automata to simulate complex spatial pattern changes. In Fuxin, the area of urban land expanded as a result of the economic transformation of this resource-based city. In response to land subsidence and other geological disasters caused by mineral resource depletion, and to improve the living environment around mining areas, the Fuxin government implemented shantytown renovation projects. These projects not only enhanced residents' living conditions but also contributed to urban land conservation. However, shantytown renovation is not a permanent solution but rather a temporary means of saving urban land. The following conclusions can be drawn: (1) Economic transformation in resource-exhausted cities inevitably drives significant changes in urban land use, with Fuxin experiencing a continuously increasing trend of urban land change from 1997 to 2010. (2) Shantytown renovation projects implemented by the Fuxin government not only improved residents' living conditions but also contributed to urban land conservation, although such measures serve as a temporary means rather than a permanent solution. (3) The CA-Markov model, integrating quantitative prediction and spatial simulation, effectively captures the spatiotemporal dynamics of urban land under human-driven factors, providing valuable support for land-use planning and economic restructuring in resource-based cities.

title: HET-Infomap: An Event- and Time-Hybrid Triggered Dynamic Sharding Method for High-Performance Blockchains
authors: Huaizhou Yang, Yalong Shi, Zhaoge Jin
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: To address the high proportion of cross-shard transactions in sharded blockchain systems and the limited flexibility of fixed time-driven resharding, this paper proposes HET- Infomap, an event- and time-hybrid triggered dynamic sharding method for high-performance blockchains. The method first constructs a directed weighted account graph from account transaction relationships and applies the Infomap community detection algorithm in directed mode to perform dynamic account sharding, thereby reducing the proportion of cross-shard transactions. It then designs a hybrid triggering mechanism that combines an event threshold with time-driven resharding, enabling the system to trigger resharding promptly when the cross-shard transaction ratio rises significantly while also preventing the shard layout from remaining fixed for too long. We implement and extend a Go- based simulator for sharded blockchain experiments and use historical Ethereum transaction data to compare Relay, Infomap, and HET-Infomap under 2-, 4-, 8-, 16-, and 20-shard settings. Experimental results show that HET-Infomap outperforms the baseline methods in throughput, transaction confirmation latency, and cross-shard transaction ratio, demonstrating better overall performance.

title: Robust Detection of Small Drones for Intelligent Anti-UAV Surveillance
authors: Lufang Liu, Zhonghang Fan
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Perception and Autonomous Control (IPAC)
abstract: Unmanned aerial vehicles (UAV) have been widely applied in various fields, and their infringement on security and privacy has also drawn public concern. Anti-UAV object detection is a significant challenge in the field of computer vision. Drone targets have small sizes, large scale variations, and are easily disturbed by background objects such as birds. These factors lead to the high false predicted ratio and false negative ratio of the relevant detection algorithm, and the poor robustness. Thus we propose two simple and effective training strategies that significantly improve detection performance without modifying the algorithm network architecture: (1) Increase the input resolution from 640 pixels to 1,280 pixels to better preserve the detailed features of small drones, and (2) Use the CopyPaste data augmentation technique to enrich the diversity of rare samples. We also propose a drone-target dataset called SmallDrone1K. This dataset focus on ultra-small scale drones and the inclusion of birds as negative samples to simulate real interference. We trained the yolov8n model using this dataset and the above strategies, and extensive experimental results show that Recall increased by 5.5%, Precision improves to 0.9801 and mean Average Precision (mAP)@[0.5:0.95] increased from 58.03% to 65.15%. These results confirm that the training strategies proposed in this paper can effectively alleviate the problem of missed detections and improve the performance of practical anti-UAV target detection task. Our work provides a practical solution that directly addresses the critical need for high-recall and robust small target detection in Anti-UAV object detection.

title: AlignFree-Net: A Registration-Free Hybrid Framework for Infrared-Visible Object Detection via Semantic Feature Matching
authors: Peng Lu, Shuntong Yu, Zeyang Li
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Multimodal fusion detection typically relies on strictly registered image pairs, imposing stringent requirements on hardware calibration and increasing deployment costs. Existing fusion methods often fail when processing unregistered source images due to the spatial misalignment of semantic features. To address this challenge, we propose AlignFree-Net, a registration-free hybrid learning framework designed for robust infrared-visible object detection. Specifically, we introduce a optimal graph matcher (OGM) based on the Kuhn-Munkres algorithm to align semantically consistent features without strict spatial correspondence. Furthermore, a detail-refining fusion module (DRF) is employed to integrate these remodeled features while preserving high-frequency details. We also present UR-Wildfire, the first benchmark dataset for unregistered multimodal smoke and wildfire detection. Extensive experiments on both the UR-Wildfire and the public M<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sup>FD datasets demonstrate that AlignFree-Net significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving superior detection accuracy even under large parallax conditions.

title: Energy Efficiency Maximization for Movable Antenna-Enabled Secure ISAC System
authors: Xiaofeng Li
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Movable antennas (MAs) have gained considerable attention due to their ability to flexibly adjust antenna positions, significantly improving the secure transmission rate of ISAC systems while maintaining sensing accuracy. Existing research on MA-assisted ISAC typically overlooks the time and energy overhead of MA movement. Thus, this paper constructs a secrecy energy efficiency (SEE) maximization problem for MA-assisted ISAC systems, considering the time and energy overhead of antenna movement. This problem is highly non-convex and has strong coupling relationships between optimization variables, making it difficult to solve. To address this issue, this paper proposes an efficient approximation algorithm based on alternating optimization. Simulation results demonstrate the rapid convergence of the proposed algorithm and significant improvement in secrecy energy efficiency performance.

title: LayoutFPL: Functional Partitioning via Large Language Model for Indoor Scene Generation
authors: Zhenpeng Tian
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Generating diverse simulation scenarios is a key technological support for advancing low-cost embodied intelligent learning in robots. Existing methods typically rely on labeled datasets, which cannot efficiently generate large and semantically consistent layouts, limiting their application in various scenarios. To address these issues, this paper proposes LayoutFPL, a scene generation framework based on a large language model. LayoutFPL divides the scene into sub-regions with independent functions, generates each sub-region independently, and then assembles them into a complete layout. LayoutFPL consists of two stages: a cascaded prediction stage and a post-processing optimization stage. In the prediction stage, the large language model generates modular layout parameters at different granularities, ensuring consistency with the features of the functional partitioning. In the post-processing optimization stage, LayoutFPL employs a hybrid strategy combining constraint satisfaction and gradient-based optimization to resolve spatial conflicts in multi-object interaction scenarios, ensuring the coherence and functionality of the layout. Compared with recently developed LayoutGPT and I-Design, LayoutFPL reduces the out-of-bounds rate by an average of 70% and the object overlap rate by 90%, while increasing the number and diversity of objects, providing a highly compatible scene generation solution for robot simulation training.

title: Design of Reflective Fresnel Plane Antenna
authors: Kedi Zhang, Chong Zhang
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Perception and Autonomous Control (IPAC)
abstract: Fresnel zone plate antennas are widely used in high-frequency communication systems due to their low profile and high gain characteristics. However, the phase compensation design of reflective Fresnel planar antennas still faces challenges related to fabrication constraints and phase control accuracy. In this work, two types of reflective Fresnel planar antennas, namely slot-type and flat-plane structures, are designed based on the phase modulation principle of Fresnel lenses. The phase delay characteristics of dielectric materials with different thicknesses are analyzed, and quarter-period and eighth-period phase compensation structures are implemented to achieve effective phase control. Full-wave electromagnetic simulations are performed to evaluate the radiation performance of the proposed antennas at 10 GHz. The results show that the quarter-period compensated Fresnel antenna achieves a gain of 22.9 dB with a beam width of approximately 11°, while the eighth-period compensated structure improves the gain to 24.4 dB and reduces the beam width to about 9°. These results demonstrate that reflective Fresnel planar antennas with optimized phase compensation provide an effective solution for high-gain microwave antenna design.

title: SIG-DETR: A Broadband Signal Detection Framework for Complex Wireless Environments
authors: Yi Xie, Minqiang Xu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: This paper addresses the problem of wideband signal detection in complex wireless environments, where large bandwidth, long signal duration, dense distribution, and cross-region targets make accurate detection difficult. To alleviate the loss of fine-grained time–frequency details caused by direct spectrogram compression, we propose a Structured Preprocessing method for Broadband Signals (SPBS), which preserves time–frequency resolution and establishes cross-block associations. A Global Edge Information Transfer (GEIT) module is further introduced to exploit shallow edge priors for multi-scale feature enhancement, thereby improving boundary awareness and mitigating target fragmentation. To obtain globally consistent predictions, a Sub-image-level Integrated Result Fusion (SIRF) strategy maps and merges sub-image detections into the global coordinate space. In addition, an Adaptive-weighted Modified Assignment Loss (AMAL) is designed to dynamically emphasize low-IoU samples and improve recall in complex scenarios. Extensive experiments on the Pioneer Cup dataset demonstrate that the proposed SIG-DETR achieves superior performance over RT-DETR and several mainstream detectors across multiple evaluation metrics, while also showing good potential for future extension toward lightweight deployment and joint detection-recognition tasks.

title: Deep Learning-Aided Robust Adaptive Beamforming for Dynamic Interference Scenarios
authors: Yisong Liu
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: Conventional robust adaptive beamforming methods generally assume that interferences are static or slowly time-varying. In rapidly changing scenarios, the latency introduced by traditional procedures, such as snapshot sampling and covariance matrix reconstruction, far exceeds the time scale of interference variations, leading to severe performance degradation. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel deep learning-aided robust adaptive beamforming method tailored for dynamic interference scenarios. The proposed approach constructs a sliding time window to generate a series of angle-time two-dimensional (2D) Capon spectrograms. These spectrograms are fed into a designed two-stage CNN-LSTM neural network. The Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) module extracts dynamic features from the 2D Capon spectrum, while the Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) module captures temporal dependencies to predict the interference direction-of-arrival (DOA) at the next time instant. The predicted directions are then utilized to pre-adaptively reconstruct the interference-plus-noise covariance matrix (IPNCM), effectively compensating for system latency. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in terms of overall performance under conditions of both gain/phase errors and signal look direction mismatch. Furthermore, it closely approaches the theoretical optimal value while exhibiting superior feasibility in rapidly time-varying scenarios.

title: High-Precision Thickness Inversion of SiC Epitaxial Layers via Multi-Beam Interferometric Spectroscopy and Nonlinear Optimization
authors: Yinglan Yan, Yilin Xu, Xinrui Zhang
publish: 2026 11th International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Signal Processing (ICSP)
abstract: The thickness of the epitaxial layer in silicon carbide (SiC) devices significantly affects their performance. However, existing measurement methods face notable limitations in terms of accuracy and applicability. To address this issue, this paper proposes a novel interferometric spectroscopy thickness inversion method, which combines DBSCAN density clustering anomaly detection and Savitzky-Golay (S-G) filtering for data preprocessing. Additionally, physical models for both dual-beam and multibeam interference are developed. For the dual-beam interference model, we derive a thickness analytical expression that includes half-wave loss and dispersion relationships, based on Snell’s law and the optical path difference of the reflected beams, which effectively handles simple light beam interference situations. In the multi-beam interference model, we employed a multi-beam interference theory based on the Airy formula, and developed a more accurate reflectance model by accounting for errors caused by linear shifts in the measured data. Experiments on SiC epitaxial wafer datasets demonstrate that our method improves measurement accuracy compared to traditional approaches. This paper provides a feasible improvement for thickness measurement in SiC devices and aims to offer valuable insights for related research and applications.

title: Joint Localization and Orientation With Triple-Beam Fingerprints in Massive MIMO-OFDM
authors: Yu Zhao, Zhenzhou Jin, Jinke Tang, Li You, Chen Sun, Xiang-Gen Xia, Xiqi Gao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Wireless Communications
abstract: With the widespread application of location-based services, fingerprint-based localization has demonstrated advantages in environments with complex signal propagation. Deep learning has significantly improved the efficiency of both offline training and online matching in localization processes. However, existing fingerprints only contain terminal position information without capturing motion states, and neural network designs have not fully incorporated structural features such as fingerprint sparsity. In this paper, we propose a triple-beam fingerprint (TBF) incorporating Doppler information and design a Transformer-based localization and orientation awareness network (LOA-Net) to simultaneously estimate user position and motion direction in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) systems. We first show the correlation between TBF and multipath information, and investigate the collinearity of different TBFs, demonstrating that TBF is an effective small-size sparse fingerprint. Then, we propose LOA-Net containing a mask-augmented detection Transformer for regression (MaskDETR-Reg) module and a fusion-enhanced Transformer for direction classification (Fusion-TDC) module to process angle-delay domain information and Doppler domain information, respectively. Finally, in the simulation of indoor scenarios defined in 3GPP 38.901, the proposed method achieves significantly better localization accuracy than weighted <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$K$ </tex-math></inline-formula>-nearest neighbors (WKNN), 2D and 3D convolutional neural networks (CNNs), and achieves satisfactory motion direction estimation accuracy.

title: SADGPR-Net: Self-Augmented Dual-Branch Network for Unsupervised Clutter Removal in Ground Penetrating Radar
authors: Jia Fu, Yesheng Gao, Mengyang Shi, Fan Yang, Xingzhao Liu
publish: IEEE Geoscience and Remote Sensing Letters
abstract: Ground Penetrating Radar (GPR) radargram are affected by antenna coupling reflections and interference from anisotropic soil media, introducing significant clutter that severely hinders subsequent target inversion and detection. Supervised learning methods rely on paired cluttered and target image, acquiring such data in real experimental scenarios is often impractical. To overcome this challenge, we design SADGPR-Net, a general unsupervised self-augmented dual-branch network designed to eliminate clutter in unpaired GPR images. We design a dual-branch parameter-sharing Decoupler–Synthesizer mechanism, which guiding the Decoupler to improve denoising performance. In the dual-branch design, Branch 2 employs the Synthesizer to generate paired data, enhancing Decoupler performance via consistency constraints and mitigating degradation from unpaired data in unsupervised networks. Branch 1 shares parameters with Branch 2 and is jointly optimized, collaboratively enhancing the Decoupler’s effectiveness. Additionally, we introduce Wasserstein loss to stabilize the training process and perceptual loss to enhance consistency. In this study, we validate the framework primarily on pipeline scenarios. Experimental results demonstrate that, compared to CycleGAN, our method improves PSNR by 7.11 dB and SSIM by 0.0148, while reducing MAE, LPIPS, DISTS, and FID by 0.45, 0.0228, 0.0691, and 34.24, respectively. These results demonstrate the superiority of our proposed method.

title:
Vertical GaN-on-GaN finFETs: Effect of SiN
                    <sub>x</sub>
                    Spacer Layer on Breakdown Voltage and Device Stability Under Gate Bias Stress
authors: Chengzhi Zhang, Aditya K. Bhat, Yuke Cao, Khaled Ahmeda, Xiang Li, Martin Kuball, Matthew D. Smith
publish: IEEE Transactions on Electron Devices
abstract: The effect of a SiN<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> spacer layer on the performance and reliability of vertical gallium nitride (GaN)-based fin field-effect-transistors (finFETs) is investigated. Inclusion of the SiN<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> spacer layer significantly enhances <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small>-state characteristics by increasing breakdown voltage from < 25 V to ~1 kV, attributed to management of electric field peaks at contact termination points, with negligible impact on <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>-state performance. However, devices with SiN<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> spacer showed significant degradation in stability of device threshold voltage following bias stress in both <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">on</small>- and <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">off</small>-states, representing reduced device reliability. This is attributed to defects in the Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub> gate dielectric layer associated with the SiN<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">x</sub> space layer deposition process, specifically a defect reservoir of density N<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}_{\text {ot}} \gt 10^{{19}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> cm<inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">${}^{-{3}}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> extending >4 nm into the gate dielectric layer according to a tunneling-front model. The performance-reliability tradeoff highlights that future fabrication methods must minimize processing damage to ensure both high-voltage capability and long-term operational stability.

title: Heterogeneous Mapping for Analog In-Memory Computing Accelerators: A Unified Workflow
authors: Corey Lammie
publish: IEEE Computer Architecture Letters
abstract: Analog In-Memory Computing (AIMC) accelerators execute matrix–vector multiplications directly within memory arrays, reducing data movement and improving DNN inference efficiency. Their limited effective precision motivates heterogeneous architectures that combine analog compute tiles with digital processing units. This letter classifies existing methods for partitioning DNN workloads across these resources by mapping granularity, optimization strategy, and model support, and distills them into a unified four-stage workflow. To demonstrate the workflow on a model class not yet addressed by existing methods, we apply its first two stages to GPT-2, producing the first AIMC-specific precision sensitivity profile for a decoder-only transformer. Sensitivity is dominated by 4 of 49 projections, with the first decoder block's attention output dominating by an order of magnitude. This suggests that projection-level mapping and selective digital execution of early-block and output-facing projections are important for reliable decoder-transformer deployment on AIMC hardware.

title: EMBC Special Issue: Modeling Early Oxygenation Trajectory in PARDS from High-Frequency Mechanical Ventilation Signals Using Deep Sequence Architectures
authors: Wenting Liu, Joseph G. Kohne, Emily Wittrup, Kayvan Najarian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Biomedical Engineering
abstract: Objective: Pediatric acute respiratory distress syndrome (PARDS) causes substantial morbidity in the pediatric intensive care unit. We investigated whether high-frequency ventilator waveforms collected during the first hour of invasive ventilation contain predictive information about subsequent oxygenation trajectory. Methods: Mechanically ventilated pediatric patients were analyzed using engineered statistical features and learned representations derived from high-frequency ventilator signals, including breath-by-breath flow waveforms from bedside ventilators. Engineered statistical features included early oxygenation indices and ventilator variables from time- and frequency-domain analyses, while learned representations were obtained from 1D-CNN embeddings with PCA-based dimensionality reduction. Two prediction tasks (12-hour OSI regression and classification of mild vs. moderate-or-higher impairment, OSI $\geq 7.5$) were evaluated using five sequence architectures (RNN, LSTM, GRU, Transformer, Mamba) across nine feature configurations, with repeated cross-validation and evaluation on held-out test and temporally separated validation cohorts. Results: For classification, the best cross-validated AUROC was $0.819 \pm 0.030$. Performance remained consistent across independent cohorts, with AUROC values of 0.787–0.823 on the test cohort and 0.808–0.832 on the temporal validation cohort, and Brier scores $\sim$0.12–0.15. For regression, the best cross-validated RMSE was $2.45 \pm 0.81$. Test RMSE ranged from 2.99 to 3.48 OSI units and validation RMSE from 2.60 to 2.85. Conclusion: High-frequency ventilator waveforms acquired during the first hour of mechanical ventilation contain measurable information about short-horizon oxygenation trajectory in PARDS. Significance: These findings demonstrate the feasibility of modeling early oxygenation trajectory using routinely available ventilator waveform data and support further prospective and multi-institutional validation prior to clinical translation.

title: FASS: Discrete Fourier Transform Adapters for Speech Separation with Incremental Learning_supp1-3700032.pdf
authors: Jie Chen
publish: N/A
abstract: Speech separation with incremental learning (SSIL), which facilitates the continuous adaptation of speech separation models to new languages, remains a critical yet underexplored area. In this paper, we propose a novel framework, termed discrete Fourier transform Adapters for Speech Separation with incremental learning (FASS), designed to mitigate catastrophic forgetting by training orthogonal Fourier-domain adapters using a parameter expansion-fusion strategy. This approach stems from our analysis of how acquiring new tasks interferes with retaining prior knowledge. Specifically, FASS converts sparse Fourier-domain parameters into dense parameter-domain weights via the inverse discrete Fourier transform (IDFT), which could preserve previous knowledge without necessitating task identifiers or additional storage. Furthermore, an extended variant, FASS-random (FASS-r), enhances scalability by randomly assigning indices to the learnable frequencies while maintaining theoretical performance. Comprehensive theoretical analyses and extensive experimental results substantiate the effectiveness of our approach.

title: Low-Complexity Beamforming and Rate Allocation for RSMA
authors: Diluka Galappaththige, Chintha Tellambura
publish: IEEE Wireless Communications Letters
abstract: This paper develops a low-complexity joint beamforming and rate allocation design for rate-splitting multiple access (RSMA) systems. Specifically, a multi-antenna base station (BS) serves multiple users to maximize the sum rate subject to common rate and transmit power constraints. The resulting problem is highly non-convex due to the coupling between beamforming and rate variables. Conventional approaches based on convex-concave procedure algorithm (CCPA) and fractional programming (FP) incur high computational complexity and scalability limitations. We propose an alternating optimization framework based on manifold optimization and the augmented Lagrangian method to solve the problem efficiently without semidefinite lifting, rank relaxation, or approximations. Numerical results show that, with 16 BS antennas, the proposed method is approximately 10× and 8× faster than CCPA and FP benchmarks, respectively, while achieving 5.2% and 3.4% sum-rate gains and higher constraint satisfaction probability.

title: Twining Stems Inspired Soft Actuators with High Aspect Ratio: Design, Characterization, and Application_supp6-3697813.doc
authors: Jingjing Feng
publish: N/A
abstract: Inspired by the helical growth strategy of natural twining stems, this study introduces a pneumatically driven Soft Twining-stem-inspired Actuator (STA) with high aspect ratio (> 20:1) and thin-walled structures. Fabricated via an integrated dip-molding and fiber-reinforced process, the STA achieves 3D spatial curling deformation with only a single pneumatic chamber, eliminating the need for multi-chamber coordination. The establishment of mechanical model identified the key parameters governing its deformation, which was then validated through performance testing and analysis. This revealed that fiber thread density regulation enable up to 67.1 mm radial contraction (33.6% length) and 1021° rotation at 80 kPa. Practical applications demonstrate STA’s versatility across multiple domains. The actuators function effectively as artificial muscles to drive a robotic elbow, and demonstrate a snap-through mechanism which facilitates rapid bidirectional motion switching. Beyond actuation applications, soft universal grippers composed of 6 STAs achieve non-destructive grasping of both common objects and irregularly shaped targets, highlighting the system’s multi-functional versatility. Furthermore, a pipe-crawling robot was constructed utilizing STAs, showcasing the ability to generate effective anisotropic friction for locomotion within confined pipelines. Overall, this study aims to establish STAs as a versatile actuator design, offering insights for simplified and high - performance soft robotics.

title: Distributed Algorithm for Constrained Optimization Over Weight-Unbalanced Digraphs
authors: Mengke Lian, Xiaoxuan Wang, Zhenyuan Guo, Shiping Wen, Tingwen Huang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: This paper addresses distributed optimization problems with compact convex set constraint and nonsmooth objectives over weight-unbalanced directed communication graphs, where the objective function is a sum of local convex functions endowed only by the corresponding agent. The weight unbalance destroys the doubly-stochastic property required by standard consensus algorithms, while the nonsmooth objectives and coupled constraints further complicate distributed computation. To tackle these challenges, we propose a novel continuous-time projection algorithm that achieves finite-time weight balancing and finite-time convergence to the feasible set from any initial values, followed by asymptotic convergence to an optimal solution. Finally, two numerical examples are performed to substantiate the effectiveness, demonstrating its strong adaptability to both weight-balanced and weight-unbalanced graphs compared with existing methods.

title: Dynamic Land Cover Mapping with Anomaly-aware Continual Learning
authors: Fengcheng Ji, Wenzhi Zhao, Ruonan Zhao, Kun Jia, Qiao Wang, Rui Peng
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Automatic land cover mapping (LCM) from remote sensing (RS) images can provide us with a comprehensive, timely, and rapid understanding of various geographical processes and phenomena on the Earth's surface. However, the existing LCM methods suffer from severe data distribution drift and lack attention to unknown anomaly detection in the context of continually updated spatiotemporal data streams. In this paper, we propose an anomaly-aware continual learning probabilistic framework (AACL) for dynamic LCM. More specifically, the AACL consists of an adaptive continual learning module and an evidence-informed unknown anomaly detection module. The architecture of the adaptive continual learning module can mitigate the influence of data distribution drift on the LCM model, making dynamic LCM possible. The evidence-informed unknown anomaly detection module enables the model to conduct trustworthy uncertainty estimation associated with each prediction result, thereby providing an anomaly score to separate unknown anomalies from known LC classes. Finally, the dynamic LCM and unknown anomaly detection capabilities are integrated into a unified probability distribution framework through uncertainty. We evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed method on two datasets spanning cross-spatial and cross-temporal scenarios, respectively. And the comparative experiments between AACL and three state-of-the-art models testify the superiority of our method in dealing with data distribution drift and unknown anomalies. The experimental results also demonstrate the potential of the proposed method for the rapid detection of unknown anomalies and provide a feasible framework for universal anomaly detection.

title: MSFFC: A novel dimensionality reduction method for improving maize nitrogen estimation from high-dimensional spectral reflectance data_supp2-3698129.xlsx
